proposals.title: Defining the Multi-Component Assembly Required for Mutant Protein-Induced Lysosomal Blockage: An Unbiased Interactome and Functional Screen
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) dysfunction, often caused by mutant protein binding to LAMP2A, is implicated in aging and neurodegenerative diseases. The prevailing model assumes a simple, stoichiometric blockade, but this fails to explain the variable severity of lysosomal dysfunction observed in different pathological contexts. A comprehensive understanding of the molecular players involved is crucial for developing targeted therapies to restore CMA flux.
proposals.hypothesis: Mutant protein-induced lysosomal blockage is not a simple LAMP2A-binding event but requires the assembly of a multi-component complex at the lysosomal membrane, involving co-receptors and regulatory factors that modulate the efficiency and consequences of the blockade.
proposals.method: We will employ proximity-dependent biotinylation (BioID) using a mutant protein-of-interest (e.g., pathogenic α-synuclein variant) targeted to the lysosomal membrane to perform unbiased interactome mapping via mass spectrometry. Interacting candidates, particularly other lysosomal membrane proteins, will be functionally validated using a targeted CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screen in a stable reporter cell line monitoring CMA flux. Hits will be analyzed for their role in modulating mutant protein binding, LAMP2A multimerization, and lysosomal stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of the protein interaction network governing mutant-induced CMA blockade, moving beyond the LAMP2A-centric view. It will identify novel therapeutic targets whose modulation could alleviate lysosomal dysfunction without disrupting basal CMA, offering a new strategy for treating CMA-related pathologies.
proposals.novelty: This approach directly challenges the canonical one-receptor model of CMA blockage by applying an oncology-inspired, systems-level framework to lysosomal biology. It integrates unbiased proteomics with a focused functional screen specifically on the lysosomal membrane compartment, a combination not yet applied to this problem.

proposals.title: Defining the Lysosomal Membrane Proteostasis Network in Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy: A Role for LAMP1 and GlialCAM Beyond LAMP2A
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway critical for cellular proteostasis, metabolism, and aging, whose decline is implicated in neurodegenerative and metabolic diseases. While LAMP2A is the established lysosomal receptor for CMA substrate translocation, the specific roles of other lysosomal membrane proteins (LMPs) in regulating CMA flux, particularly in pathological blockages, remain undefined. This gap limits our ability to develop targeted CMA modulators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA blockage in disease-associated mutants involves the dysregulated function or interaction of specific LMPs beyond LAMP2A, which are essential for the assembly or stability of the CMA translocation complex.
proposals.method: We will employ a CRISPR/Cas9-based dual screening approach in CMA reporter cell lines. First, a genome-wide screen will identify LMPs whose loss rescues or exacerbates CMA blockage in cells expressing mutant CMA substrates (e.g., mutant α-synuclein). Second, a focused, arrayed siRNA screen targeting all known LMPs will validate hits and assess their role in wild-type CMA. Biochemical validation will include co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays to map interactions between LAMP2A and candidate LMPs (e.g., LAMP1, GlialCAM) in normal and blocked CMA states, and in vitro reconstitution assays using purified lysosomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the essential lysosomal membrane 'proteostasis network' for CMA, identifying novel regulatory components. It will establish whether CMA blockages are purely substrate-receptor driven or involve broader membrane complex dysfunction, providing new mechanistic insights into CMA failure in aging and disease.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on LAMP2A expression and substrate recognition, this proposal systematically tests the non-obvious premise that other LMPs are integral to CMA function and that their perturbation underlies specific pathological blockages, moving beyond a LAMP2A-centric view.

proposals.title: Genetic Dissociation of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Inhibition from Mutant-Induced Toxicity Reveals a Gain-of-Function Pathogenic Mechanism
proposals.motivation: Mutations in CMA-related proteins, such as LAMP2A, are linked to neurodegenerative and aging-related diseases. The prevailing model attributes pathology primarily to the loss of CMA function and proteostasis collapse. However, this model fails to fully explain the specific cellular toxicity profiles and may overlook separable, targetable disease mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that disease-associated CMA mutants possess a toxic gain-of-function activity that is genetically and pharmacologically separable from their inhibitory effect on CMA flux.
proposals.method: We will establish a stable cell model (e.g., mouse embryonic fibroblasts) expressing a well-characterized toxic CMA mutant (e.g., a pathogenic LAMP2A variant) and a dual reporter system for CMA flux and cell viability. Using this model, we will perform a genome-wide CRISPR/Cas9 knockout screen to identify genetic modifiers that rescue cell death while CMA activity remains suppressed. Hits will be validated in primary neuronal cultures and assessed for their ability to uncouple toxicity from CMA blockade using orthogonal assays, including lysosomal proteolysis and TUNEL staining.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence for a gain-of-function mechanism in CMA-related diseases, fundamentally reframing their etiology. It will identify novel genetic nodes and pathways that specifically modulate mutant toxicity, revealing therapeutic targets distinct from CMA restoration strategies.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying a conceptual framework from prion biology—dissociating infectivity from toxicity—to the CMA field. It moves beyond the loss-of-function paradigm to systematically search for a separable toxic activity, which has not been attempted for CMA mutants.

proposals.title: Proteomic Profiling of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Substrate Flux to Distinguish Loss-of-Function from Gain-of-Function Toxic Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) dysfunction is implicated in aging and neurodegenerative diseases, but the pathogenic mechanisms remain unclear. Some disease-linked CMA-related mutants may exert toxicity not merely by blocking CMA but through a distinct gain-of-function, a hypothesis difficult to test without a comprehensive map of CMA substrate flux. Dissecting these mechanisms is crucial for developing precise therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that expression of a toxic CMA-related mutant (e.g., a pathogenic LAMP2A variant) will lead to the accumulation of a unique subset of proteins beyond those that accumulate upon general CMA inhibition, and this mutant-specific proteomic signature drives gain-of-function toxicity.
proposals.method: We will perform quantitative tandem mass tag (TMT) proteomics in a validated cellular model (e.g., mouse fibroblasts) under three conditions: 1) basal, 2) CMA inhibition (via LAMP2A knockdown or the CMA inhibitor P140), and 3) expression of a toxic CMA mutant. Proteins with significantly increased abundance in both inhibition and mutant conditions will be classified as general CMA substrates. Proteins accumulating exclusively in the mutant condition will be identified as the gain-of-function signature, using statistical analysis (ANOVA with post-hoc tests, FDR correction) and bioinformatics (STRING, GO enrichment).
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comparative, proteome-wide map of CMA substrate flux under loss-of-function versus putative gain-of-function conditions. It will directly test the gain-of-function toxicity hypothesis and identify novel candidate protein mediators, providing a mechanistic framework to reinterpret the role of CMA mutants in disease pathogenesis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identified CMA substrates, this comparative flux analysis is novel for directly isolating a mutant-specific toxic signature, moving beyond cataloging substrates to defining distinct pathogenic mechanisms.

proposals.title: Kinetic Profiling of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Reveals Substrate-Specific Import Deficits in LAMP2A and HSC70 Mutants
proposals.motivation: Mutations in core CMA machinery, such as LAMP2A and HSC70, are linked to neurodegenerative diseases and aging-related pathologies. However, it is unknown whether these mutations cause a uniform block to all CMA substrates or selectively impair the import of specific protein classes, which would have profound implications for understanding disease specificity and developing targeted therapies. Current assays, like the KFERQ-GFP reporter, provide a binary readout that cannot resolve these kinetic and selectivity nuances.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pathogenic mutations in CMA machinery components do not cause a uniform global import defect but instead create substrate-selective kinetic bottlenecks, disproportionately affecting substrates with specific structural or biophysical properties.
proposals.method: We will construct a multiplexed library of 20-30 fluorescent CMA reporter substrates by fusing distinct protein domains (e.g., from GAPDH, RNASE1, MEF2D, α-synuclein) to spectrally separable fluorescent proteins, each containing a validated CMA-targeting motif. Using CRISPR-edited isogenic cell lines harboring disease-relevant LAMP2A or HSC70 mutations, we will perform live-cell imaging with high temporal resolution to track the lysosomal translocation kinetics of each substrate simultaneously. Kinetic parameters (import rate, lag time) will be extracted via automated image analysis and compared statistically across substrate classes and mutant backgrounds using multivariate ANOVA.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative, high-resolution map of CMA substrate import kinetics, moving the field beyond binary blockage assays. It will define whether CMA mutations cause global or substrate-selective defects, providing a mechanistic basis for the specific proteostatic vulnerabilities observed in associated diseases. The substrate library will serve as a new community resource for probing CMA function.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies CMA activation or inhibition, this study is the first to apply multiplexed kinetic profiling to dissect the selectivity of import defects, a critical but unexplored layer of CMA regulation. The approach integrates live-cell imaging of a diverse physiological substrate library, a significant advance over single-reporter or endpoint biochemical assays.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis of LAMP2A Mutant Binding Kinetics Reveals Determinants of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway crucial for proteostasis, metabolism, and immune function, as evidenced by its decline in aging and its activation by calorie restriction. The lysosomal receptor LAMP2A is the rate-limiting component of CMA, but the precise biophysical parameters governing its interaction with substrate proteins remain poorly defined. Quantifying how disease-associated or functionally relevant LAMP2A mutations alter binding kinetics is essential to understand CMA dysregulation at a mechanistic level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific mutations in the cytosolic tail of LAMP2A will differentially alter its binding affinity (Kd) and dwell time for canonical CMA substrates, and that these kinetic parameters will directly predict the functional efficiency of CMA flux in living cells.
proposals.method: We will employ single-molecule total internal reflection fluorescence (smTIRF) microscopy to quantify the binding kinetics of purified, fluorescently tagged CMA substrates (e.g., GAPDH, RNase A) to LAMP2A variants reconstituted in supported lipid bilayers. A panel of LAMP2A mutants will be tested, including those mimicking post-translational modifications and disease-associated variants. Binding events will be tracked and analyzed using custom particle-tracking algorithms (e.g., uTrack) and hidden Markov modeling to extract precise on-rates, off-rates, and dwell times.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative kinetic map linking LAMP2A structure to substrate binding, a fundamental gap in CMA biology. It will provide a biophysical framework to predict the functional impact of LAMP2A variants found in aging and disease, informing the rational design of CMA modulators.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has shown LAMP2A is essential for CMA, a direct, quantitative measurement of the binding kinetics of defined LAMP2A mutants at the single-molecule level has not been performed. This approach moves beyond correlative cellular assays to establish causative kinetic principles.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Critical Impairment Threshold of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy in Alpha-Synuclein Aggregation Using a Traffic Flow Analogy
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a key pathway for degrading alpha-synuclein, and its dysfunction is implicated in Parkinson's disease. However, it remains unclear whether CMA decline is merely correlative or a direct causal driver of the pathological phase transition to Lewy body formation. Determining a causal threshold is critical for developing targeted therapies aimed at restoring proteostasis before irreversible aggregation occurs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neuronal proteostasis network exhibits a critical point, analogous to traffic flow, where a defined level of CMA impairment creates a bottleneck sufficient to trigger a non-linear shift from normal alpha-synuclein clearance to sustained aggregation.
proposals.method: We will model the proteostasis network using a system of ordinary differential equations, treating CMA as a clearance lane with a defined capacity. Parameters for alpha-synuclein synthesis, CMA, and alternative degradation pathways (e.g., proteasome, macroautophagy) will be derived from pre-2026 literature and calibrated in SH-SY5Y neuronal cell models. The model will be used to simulate the relationship between CMA activity (density) and aggregate accumulation (flow/jam). We will then experimentally validate predictions by titrating CMA inhibition (e.g., using LAMP2A knockdown or dominant-negative HSC70 constructs) and measuring the precise threshold for alpha-synuclein aggregation via quantitative immunofluorescence and filter trap assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from qualitative associations to a quantitative framework defining the causal relationship between CMA decline and pathology. It will identify a specific, targetable threshold of CMA activity for therapeutic intervention in Parkinson's disease and related synucleinopathies.
proposals.novelty: The application of traffic flow theory to model proteostasis is a novel conceptual framework. This approach uniquely seeks to define a precise, causal impairment threshold for CMA, moving beyond established correlations between general CMA decline and aggregate presence.

proposals.title: Mapping the Hierarchical Competition of Substrates at the LAMP2A Pore Reveals the Molecular Determinants of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Selectivity
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is crucial for proteostasis and its dysfunction is implicated in aging and neurodegenerative diseases. However, the field lacks a mechanistic understanding of how the single LAMP2A pore handles multiple substrates, and how pathogenic proteins like mutant alpha-synuclein block CMA. Distinguishing primary substrates from secondary accumulations is essential to understand disease pathology and design targeted therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA substrates compete for binding and translocation at the LAMP2A pore in a hierarchical manner defined by specific recognition motifs and binding kinetics, and that pathogenic blockers like mutant alpha-synuclein selectively displace a subset of high-affinity substrates, causing a cascade of secondary proteostatic disruptions.
proposals.method: We will first use a multiplexed, live-cell proteomic strategy (e.g., tandem mass tag-based mass spectrometry) to identify proteins that accumulate upon CMA inhibition by mutant alpha-synuclein. We will then employ single-molecule Total Internal Reflection Fluorescence (TIRF) microscopy to visualize the binding of fluorescently tagged candidate substrates and the mutant blocker to LAMP2A at the lysosomal membrane of living cells. This platform will be used for real-time competitive binding assays, quantifying kinetic parameters (on/off rates, dwell times) to establish a hierarchy of substrate displacement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first kinetic map of substrate competition at the LAMP2A pore, definitively identifying primary CMA substrates versus proteins that accumulate secondarily. It will establish a molecular hierarchy of CMA recognition, offering a new framework to understand how specific blockers contribute to disease and revealing potential therapeutic nodes to restore selective CMA flux.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of proteomic discovery with single-molecule functional validation in live cells to deconvolute the mechanism of CMA blockage, moving beyond correlative accumulation lists to a causal, kinetic model of pore-level competition.

proposals.title: A Computational Screen for Mutation-Dependent Substrates of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Reveals Novel Drivers of Proteostasis Collapse
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway crucial for cellular proteostasis, and its dysfunction is linked to aging and neurodegenerative diseases. While mutations in CMA substrates like alpha-synuclein can impair their recognition by the lysosomal receptor LAMP2A (LGP96), a systematic understanding of how disease-associated mutations in other proteins alter their CMA targeting is lacking. This gap limits our ability to predict which mutations might cause pathology through aberrant CMA engagement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that disease-associated mutations in cytosolic proteins can create or disrupt cryptic CMA-targeting motifs, and that computational protein-protein docking can predict these mutation-specific changes in binding affinity to the LAMP2A luminal domain, identifying novel pathological CMA substrates.
proposals.method: We will employ the HADDOCK2.4 software, which excels at modeling protein-protein interactions using experimental and bioinformatics data as restraints. We will dock structural models of wild-type versus mutant forms of candidate proteins from a curated library (including known CMA substrates and neurodegeneration-linked proteins) to the solved structure of the human LAMP2A luminal domain. Docking simulations will be performed in triplicate, and binding poses and affinity scores (HADDOCK scores) will be analyzed to rank proteins by mutation-induced binding changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first predictive, mutation-aware map of potential CMA substrates, moving beyond known interactions. It will provide a ranked list of high-priority candidate proteins whose pathogenic mutations likely operate via CMA dysregulation, offering new mechanistic insights into diseases of proteostasis. The validated computational pipeline will serve as a discovery tool for the field.
proposals.novelty: This approach innovates by using docking not to analyze a single known complex, but as a high-throughput discovery screen to identify *new* mutation-dependent CMA substrates, a concept not previously applied to this pathway. It directly integrates Dr. Cuervo's expertise in CMA selectivity with computational structural biology to predict pathological interactions.

proposals.title: Proteome-wide kinetic mapping of chaperone-mediated autophagy substrates using multiplexed temporal SILAC.
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway crucial for proteostasis, metabolism, and aging, with its dysfunction linked to neurodegenerative and metabolic diseases. However, the full repertoire of CMA substrates remains poorly defined, limiting our understanding of its physiological roles and therapeutic potential. Current methods for identifying substrates are low-throughput or indirect, creating a critical need for a systematic, kinetic approach to map the CMA-dependent proteome.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a multiplexed, temporal pulse-chase proteomic strategy will enable the system-wide identification and kinetic profiling of proteins whose degradation is specifically dependent on functional CMA, revealing a comprehensive network of CMA substrates.
proposals.method: We will adapt multi-tracer stable isotope labeling principles to cell biology using a 6-plex SILAC (stable isotope labeling by amino acids in cell culture) scheme. Cells in distinct isotopic states will be treated with a validated CMA inhibitor (e.g., targeting LAMP2A) at staggered time points, followed by a chase period in heavy media. Quantitative mass spectrometry will measure protein accumulation kinetics upon CMA blockage. Data analysis will employ specialized algorithms, including linear mixed-effects modeling for kinetic curve fitting and significance analysis of time-course data, to distinguish bona fide CMA substrates from indirect effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive, kinetic map of the CMA substrate landscape, providing a foundational resource for the field. It will identify novel CMA-dependent proteins and pathways, offering mechanistic insights into CMA's role in cellular physiology and its contribution to age-related diseases, thereby opening new avenues for therapeutic targeting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual translation of ecological multi-tracer pulse-labeling to proteostasis and the technical implementation of temporal multiplexing to deconvolute CMA-specific degradation kinetics proteome-wide, a significant advance over existing static or candidate-based approaches.

proposals.title: Proteome-wide identification of CMA substrates reveals a network of proteins regulating immune function and cellular proteostasis
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway crucial for proteostasis, metabolism, and aging. While individual CMA substrates are known, a comprehensive proteomic map of CMA-dependent proteins is lacking, limiting our understanding of how CMA dysfunction contributes to age-related diseases and immune decline, as suggested by recent findings linking LAMP2A restoration to improved T cell function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acute genetic or pharmacological blockage of CMA will lead to the specific accumulation of a distinct network of proteins beyond known substrates, and that this network will be enriched for regulators of immune cell function and proteostatic stress responses.
proposals.method: We will use a stable cell line expressing a doxycycline-inducible shRNA against LAMP2A to achieve acute CMA blockage, coupled with dynamic SILAC (Stable Isotope Labeling by Amino acids in Cell culture) proteomics. Proteins exhibiting increased half-lives upon LAMP2A knockdown will be identified using MaxQuant software for quantification and statistical analysis. Candidate substrates will be validated using established CMA assays, including in vitro lysosomal uptake and KFERQ-motif analysis via the CMA predictor algorithm CMAp.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first unbiased, proteome-wide catalog of CMA substrates, defining the 'CMA degradome.' It will provide mechanistic insight into how CMA sustains immune cell proteostasis, directly linking specific substrate accumulation to the T cell dysregulation observed in aging. The dataset will serve as a foundational resource for identifying novel therapeutic targets for age-related diseases.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous studies focused on individual substrates, this proposal uses a dynamic proteomic screen to systematically identify the full spectrum of CMA-dependent proteins, directly connecting pathway blockage to specific proteostatic and immune consequences in an unbiased manner.

proposals.title: Cell-Autonomous vs. Circuit-Level Pathogenesis: Linking Single-Neuron Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Activity to Functional Synaptic Output in Dopaminergic Circuits
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) activity declines with age and is implicated in neurodegenerative diseases like Parkinson's, where dopaminergic neurons are vulnerable. Pre-2026 work by Cuervo and others shows CMA is crucial for neuronal proteostasis, but it remains unknown whether CMA dysfunction causes pathology primarily through cell-autonomous failure of the neuron or by disrupting its specific role in a functional circuit. Resolving this is critical for designing targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA dysfunction in dopaminergic neurons leads to pathology not solely through intracellular proteotoxicity, but by specifically impairing synaptic transmission and altering the neuron's influence on defined local microcircuits, thereby disrupting network motifs essential for motor control.
proposals.method: We will combine single-neuron CMA profiling with dual patch-clamp electrophysiology and optogenetic circuit mapping in acute midbrain slices from CMA reporter mice (e.g., LAMP2A-GFP) and CMA-deficient models (e.g., LAMP2A knockdown). Following paired recordings to assess synaptic transmission, the patched neuron will be harvested for single-cell qPCR of CMA components (LAMP2A, HSC70) and substrates (e.g., MEF2D, α-synuclein). Concurrently, we will use channelrhodopsin-assisted circuit mapping (CRACM) to map functional inputs onto the recorded neuron and assess its output onto postsynaptic targets, correlating CMA status with functional connectivity metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, functional link between the molecular state of CMA in a defined neuron and its real-time synaptic and circuit-level performance. It will establish a causal framework to distinguish cell-autonomous from circuit-mediated mechanisms in CMA-linked neurodegeneration. The findings will guide therapeutic strategies toward bolstering CMA in specific cell types or, alternatively, toward circuit-level neuromodulation.
proposals.novelty: While CMA's role in neuronal survival is known, directly measuring its activity in a single neuron immediately after assessing its functional synaptic connectivity is a novel, high-resolution approach. This moves beyond correlative studies to test causality between a specific degradative blockage and systems-level dysfunction in a defined circuit.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Microfluidic Analysis of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Heterogeneity in Dopaminergic Neurons
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is crucial for neuronal proteostasis, and its dysfunction is implicated in Parkinson's disease, where dopaminergic neurons are selectively vulnerable. However, CMA activity is currently measured in bulk cell populations, masking critical cell-to-cell variability that could define neuronal susceptibility. Understanding this heterogeneity is essential to identify the earliest proteostatic failures in neurodegeneration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual dopaminergic neurons exhibit a wide, functionally significant range of basal CMA activity, and that this heterogeneity determines their differential vulnerability to proteotoxic stress from mutant alpha-synuclein.
proposals.method: We will adapt single-cell patch-clamp principles to biochemistry by designing a microfluidic platform with nanoscale chambers to isolate individual primary dopaminergic neurons. Each chamber will allow precise perfusion control to introduce CMA substrates and inhibitors. CMA activity will be quantified in real-time using ultra-sensitive, genetically encoded reporters based on the KFERQ motif, such as a photo-convertible CMA substrate (e.g., Dendra2-KFERQ) whose lysosomal degradation is tracked via fluorescence loss, combined with lysosomal pH reporters (e.g., Lyso-pHluorin) to confirm uptake.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first method for quantifying real-time CMA flux in single living neurons, directly linking CMA heterogeneity to neuronal vulnerability. It will provide a foundational platform for screening CMA modulators at single-cell resolution and define the proteostatic landscape of neurons at risk in Parkinson's disease.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying the spatial isolation and environmental control of patch-clamp electrophysiology to the biochemical measurement of a specific autophagic pathway in single cells, moving beyond population averages to resolve functional heterogeneity.

proposals.title: Single-neuron functional mapping of the LAMP2A substrate recognition motif in dopaminergic neurons reveals determinants of chaperone-mediated autophagy flux.
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is crucial for neuronal proteostasis, and its decline is implicated in Parkinson's disease. While the canonical KFERQ-like motif is known for CMA targeting, the precise structural determinants for functional engagement by the lysosomal receptor LAMP2A (LGP96) within the complex neuronal environment remain poorly defined. A granular, single-cell understanding is needed to explain how disease-associated mutations in substrates or LAMP2A itself disrupt selective neuronal proteolysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific physicochemical features within the KFERQ-like motif, beyond the canonical sequence, dictate the efficiency of substrate binding to LAMP2A and subsequent CMA flux in individual neurons, and that disease-associated mutations disrupt these critical motif features.
proposals.method: We will engineer a library of CMA reporter substrates (e.g., GAPDH-CMA-mCherry) containing systematically mutated putative binding motifs, focusing on charge, hydrophobicity, and residue position. Using single-cell nucleofection, we will introduce these variants into primary mouse dopaminergic neurons and quantify CMA activity via a validated fluorescence-based reporter (Kaushik & Cuervo, 2012) combined with high-content imaging and automated single-neuron analysis. Substrate turnover rates will be correlated with motif variants to generate a functional binding map. Parallel in vitro binding assays with purified LAMP2A will validate biophysical interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first high-resolution, functional map of the LAMP2A substrate recognition motif within its native neuronal context. It will identify which motif features are essential for CMA flux in vivo, providing a mechanistic basis for interpreting the pathogenicity of disease-associated mutations in CMA substrates. The single-neuron approach will reveal cell-to-cell heterogeneity in CMA processing capacity.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond in vitro binding assays and bulk tissue analysis by directly linking motif structure to functional CMA flux at the single-neuron level, a resolution previously unattained. It uniquely integrates systematic motif mutagenesis with single-cell CMA activity profiling in a vulnerable neuronal population.

proposals.title: Engineered Bifunctional Adaptors Couple Disaggregation to Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy for Targeted Clearance of Pathogenic Aggregates
proposals.motivation: Age-related neurodegenerative diseases are characterized by the accumulation of toxic protein aggregates (e.g., amyloid-β, α-synuclein) that are poorly degraded. Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway that declines with age, contributing to proteotoxicity. While CMA activation is beneficial, its inherent substrate selectivity prevents it from directly engaging large, structured aggregates, creating a critical therapeutic barrier.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineered bifunctional adaptor molecules, which simultaneously bind specific aggregate components and the CMA receptor LAMP2A, can forcibly recruit the CMA machinery to aggregate surfaces, and that co-activation of CMA with a potent disaggregase will create a synergistic 'disaggregate-and-degrade' system to clear otherwise refractory aggregates.
proposals.method: We will design and validate bifunctional adaptors using a nanobody or scFv domain targeting amyloid-β or α-synuclein, fused to a high-affinity CMA-targeting motif (e.g., a pentapeptide KFERQ-like sequence optimized for LAMP2A binding). These adaptors will be co-expressed with core CMA components (LAMP2A, HSC70) and a heterologous disaggregase complex (e.g., the Hsp104 disaggregase from yeast, or human Hsp110/Hsp70/Hsp40 systems) in cellular models of protein aggregation. Clearance will be quantified via immunofluorescence, filter trap assays, and lysosomal flux measurements using established CMA reporters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, targeted protein clearance strategy that forcibly engages CMA against specific pathogenic aggregates. It will provide proof-of-concept for overcoming the substrate specificity limitation of CMA and demonstrate the therapeutic potential of coupling disaggregation with selective lysosomal degradation. The findings could define a new platform for degrading a range of aggregation-prone proteins in aging and disease.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel because it uses engineered adaptors to re-target CMA to substrates it normally ignores, and it mechanistically couples a disaggregase activity directly to the CMA translocation step, creating a concerted clearance mechanism not found in nature or prior therapeutic strategies.

proposals.title: In vivo analysis of chaperone-mediated autophagy dysfunction and restoration in transgenic mouse models of alpha-synucleinopathy
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a key selective degradation pathway for cytosolic proteins, including alpha-synuclein. Pre-2026 work has established that CMA activity declines with age and that mutant forms of alpha-synuclein, such as A53T and A30P, can act as CMA substrates and inhibitors, potentially contributing to Parkinson's disease pathogenesis. However, the in vivo dynamics of CMA function, its specific failure points, and the systemic consequences of its restoration in established synucleinopathy remain poorly characterized in live animal models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transgenic mouse models expressing mutant alpha-synuclein (A53T, A30P) will exhibit tissue-specific and age-dependent impairments in CMA flux, and that genetic restoration of core CMA components (e.g., LAMP2A) will mitigate proteotoxicity, improve neuronal health, and ameliorate motor phenotypes.
proposals.method: We will employ transgenic mouse models (e.g., Thy1-h[A53T]α-syn) and age-matched controls. CMA activity will be analyzed in vivo using a validated fluorescent reporter (KFERQ-PS-CFP2) delivered via AAV vectors to the substantia nigra and cortex. We will quantify CMA flux via confocal microscopy and biochemical assays for lysosomal uptake. Parallel cohorts will undergo genetic restoration of CMA via viral-mediated LAMP2A overexpression. Phenotypic assessment will include longitudinal motor behavioral tests (rotarod, pole test), immunohistochemistry for pathological alpha-synuclein (pS129), and analysis of T cell compartments in peripheral lymphoid organs, given the established link between CMA and immune function in aging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, longitudinal in vivo map of CMA dysfunction in a synucleinopathy model, linking neuronal proteostasis failure to systemic immune alterations. It will directly test the therapeutic potential of CMA augmentation, moving beyond correlative studies to establish causality. The findings will clarify whether CMA failure is a driver or a consequence of pathology.
proposals.novelty: While CMA's role in degrading alpha-synuclein is known in vitro, this proposal uniquely integrates in vivo CMA flux measurement with targeted restoration and a systemic analysis of both neuronal and immune outcomes in the same animal model, a multi-system approach not previously undertaken.

proposals.title: Immunomagnetic Isolation and Proteomic Profiling of Dysfunctional Lysosomes in Models of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Impairment
proposals.motivation: Lysosomal dysfunction is a hallmark of aging and neurodegenerative diseases, often involving specific blockages in pathways like chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA). Current methods analyze whole-cell lysates or crude organelle fractions, obscuring the specific molecular composition of the dysfunctional organelle subpopulation. A technique to directly isolate 'stuck' lysosomes is needed to define the precise nature of these blockages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lysosomes with luminal or membrane-associated blockages, such as those accumulating CMA substrates due to LAMP2A deficiency, will co-precipitate with antibody-coated magnetic beads targeting LAMP1, enabling their biochemical isolation from functional lysosomes.
proposals.method: We will conjugate anti-LAMP1 antibodies to magnetic beads and apply them to crude lysosomal fractions from wild-type and CMA-deficient (e.g., LAMP2A knockdown) cells or tissues. Following magnetic capture and stringent washing, the isolated lysosomes will be analyzed by quantitative mass spectrometry to identify enriched proteins. We will compare the proteome of beads from mutant vs. wild-type samples to distinguish the blockage-specific 'interactome' from background binding.
proposals.contribution: This will establish a novel method for the direct isolation and molecular characterization of a defined dysfunctional organelle state. It will provide an unprecedented proteomic signature of CMA-blocked lysosomes, identifying new components of the pathological aggregates and potential therapeutic targets to restore lysosomal clearance.
proposals.novelty: While immunomagnetic separation is standard for cells, its adaptation to isolate a specific functional state of an organelle based on co-precipitation of blockage complexes is novel. This moves beyond simple organelle isolation to functional-state-specific organelle proteomics.

proposals.title: Direct Proteomic and Functional Profiling of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy-Deficient Lysosomes Isolated from Treg Cells
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is crucial for selective protein degradation and cellular homeostasis, and its dysfunction is implicated in aging and immune decline. While CMA impairment is linked to lysosomal blockage, the specific protein cargoes that accumulate and directly compromise lysosomal function in physiologically relevant cells, like regulatory T (Treg) cells, remain poorly characterized at the organellar level. Direct isolation and analysis of these 'stuck' lysosomes is needed to move beyond correlative studies and define the primary pathogenic substrates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lysosomes isolated from Treg cells with genetically or pharmacologically inhibited CMA will be enriched for a specific subset of non-degraded proteins, including key immunoregulatory factors, and that this cargo directly impairs lysosomal acidification and degradative capacity.
proposals.method: We will employ magnetic immuno-isolation of LAMP2A-positive lysosomes from wild-type and CMA-deficient (e.g., LAMP2A knockdown) Treg cells. Proteomic analysis (LC-MS/MS) of isolated lysosomes will identify enriched 'stuck' proteins. Functional assays on the isolated organelle fraction will measure intra-lysosomal pH using LysoSensor and in vitro degradation capacity using fluorogenic substrates. Validation will involve reintroducing candidate proteins via electroporation into purified wild-type lysosomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct proteomic catalog of proteins that physically block CMA-deficient lysosomes in a key immune cell type. It will establish a causal link between specific substrate accumulation and organelle dysfunction, offering mechanistic insight into how CMA failure in Treg cells may drive immune aging. The developed isolation and functional assay platform will be a resource for studying organellar pathology.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in moving from cellular CMA activity measurements to the direct functional and compositional analysis of the affected organelle itself from a specific, disease-relevant primary cell type (Treg cells), allowing for unambiguous identification of pathogenic substrates rather than inferred candidates.

proposals.title: Selective Clearance of Intracellular Aβ via Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Modulates T Cell Dysfunction and Ameliorates Alzheimer's Disease Pathology
proposals.motivation: Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathology is characterized by extracellular Aβ plaques, but pre-2026 evidence points to intraneuronal Aβ accumulation in autophagic vacuoles (AVs) as an early and toxic event. The relative contribution of this intracellular reservoir versus extracellular plaques to immune dysfunction and neurodegeneration remains unclear. This is critical because current therapeutic strategies largely target extracellular Aβ with limited clinical success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intracellular Aβ pool, particularly within AVs, is the primary pathogenic driver of AD-related immune dysfunction and synaptic toxicity, and that selective clearance of this pool via chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) will be more effective than targeting extracellular Aβ.
proposals.method: We will use a novel inducible mouse model (e.g., APP/PS1) with a CMA reporter (LAMP2A-HA) and T cell-specific fluorescent tags to track CMA activity and immune cell states. Intracellular Aβ will be selectively targeted using a cell-penetrating, CMA-activating peptide (derived from Hsc70), while extracellular Aβ will be targeted with a peripherally restricted anti-Aβ antibody. We will employ high-resolution confocal microscopy and 3D electron microscopy to quantify Aβ in AVs and synapses, and use spectral flow cytometry to analyze T cell compartments (focusing on Treg and effector subsets) in brain and spleen. Behavioral and synaptic function will be assessed longitudinally.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the pathogenic primacy of intracellular versus extracellular Aβ pools in vivo. It will establish a causal link between intracellular Aβ clearance via CMA, restoration of immune function (particularly Tregs), and amelioration of pathology, providing a mechanistic rationale for CMA-boosting therapies in AD.
proposals.novelty: While CMA's role in protein degradation is known, its specific function in clearing pathogenic intracellular Aβ and the downstream consequences for the adaptive immune system in AD are unexplored. This integrates Dr. Cuervo's expertise in CMA with the emerging pre-2026 concept of neuro-immune crosstalk in neurodegeneration.

proposals.title: Cell-Type Specificity of Autophagic Flux Impairment in the Aging Murine Brain
proposals.motivation: Age-related decline in chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) and macroautophagy contributes to neurodegeneration and proteinopathies. However, studies often treat the brain as a homogeneous tissue, obscuring whether autophagic dysfunction is a global phenomenon or originates in specific, vulnerable cell populations. Determining this cellular specificity is crucial for developing targeted therapies to restore proteostasis in aging and diseases like Alzheimer's.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the age-related block in autophagic maturation is not uniform but exhibits significant cell-type specificity, with neurons showing a more profound impairment in lysosomal clearance compared to glial cells such as astrocytes and microglia.
proposals.method: We will employ fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) to isolate pure populations of neurons (NeuN+), astrocytes (GFAP+), and microglia (CD11b+) from the cortices of young (6-month) and aged (24-month) CMA reporter mice (expressing KFERQ-PA-mCherry-EGFP). Autophagic flux will be quantified via flow cytometry of isolated cells by measuring the mCherry/EGFP signal ratio, where a low ratio indicates efficient lysosomal degradation. Parallel immunohistochemistry on brain sections will assess colocalization of LAMP2A, LC3-II, and p62 with cell-type-specific markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a cell-type-resolved map of autophagic dysfunction in the aging brain, directly testing the 'neuron-specific defect' model. It will identify which cellular compartments are most vulnerable to proteostatic collapse, informing whether therapeutic strategies (e.g., LAMP2A restoration) should be broadly applied or neuron-targeted.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Cuervo's lab has shown systemic CMA decline and immune benefits of LAMP2A restoration, a direct, quantitative comparison of autophagic flux across distinct brain cell types from the same aged animal has not been performed, moving beyond bulk tissue analysis.

proposals.title: Cargo-specific autophagic vacuoles drive neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease models
proposals.motivation: In Alzheimer's disease (AD), a hallmark is the massive accumulation of autophagic vacuoles (AVs) in dystrophic neurites, which is thought to result from a failure in autophagic clearance. However, it is unknown whether this bulk accumulation is uniformly toxic or if specific, stalled AVs containing particular protein aggregates or damaged organelles are the primary drivers of neuronal dysfunction. Resolving this is critical for developing targeted therapies that address the pathogenic core of autophagic failure, rather than just its downstream consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that only a specific subset of autophagic vacuoles, defined by their cargo (e.g., phosphorylated tau oligomers, damaged mitochondria, or specific CMA substrates), is causally linked to synaptic toxicity and neurodegeneration in AD, while the majority of AVs are relatively inert.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach using AD mouse models (e.g., 5xFAD, tauopathy) and human post-mortem brain tissue. First, we will use immuno-electron microscopy and super-resolution imaging to molecularly profile the cargo within individual AVs in vulnerable neurons. Second, leveraging Dr. Cuervo's expertise in chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), we will use a novel, inducible LAMP2A-KFERQ-Dendra2 reporter system to selectively label and track the fate of CMA substrates within AVs. Third, we will employ a 'selective autophagy' toolkit, using siRNA knockdown of specific autophagy receptors (e.g., p62, OPTN, NBR1) to determine if blocking the sequestration of specific cargoes (like damaged mitochondria via mitophagy) reduces the formation of pathogenic AVs and rescues synaptic and cognitive deficits.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond the simplistic view of autophagic 'blockade' by identifying the specific pathogenic cargoes within AVs that are responsible for neuronal toxicity. It will establish a direct link between selective autophagy failure for specific substrates and AD pathogenesis, providing novel, high-value therapeutic targets (e.g., specific autophagy receptors or cargoes) for intervention.
proposals.novelty: While bulk AV accumulation is a known feature of AD, the concept that pathogenicity is cargo-specific and not a generic property of all AVs is novel. This proposal uniquely integrates Dr. Cuervo's CMA-focused tools with broader selective autophagy methodologies to dissect AV heterogeneity at a single-organelle level in a disease context.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Regulation of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy in Neuronal Compartments: A Microfluidic Analysis
proposals.motivation: Neurons are uniquely vulnerable to the age-related decline of chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), a selective degradation pathway for cytosolic proteins. Pre-2026 work, including from the Cuervo lab, shows CMA dysfunction contributes to neurodegenerative disease, but the spatial dynamics of CMA in axons and dendrites—critical for synaptic integrity and protein quality control—remain unexplored due to technical limitations in compartmentalizing neuronal processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA activity and substrate selectivity are differentially regulated between the somatodendritic and axonal compartments, and that this compartment-specific regulation is disrupted in models of neurodegeneration and aging.
proposals.method: We will employ microfluidic chambers to physically isolate axons from neuronal cell bodies. Using primary hippocampal neurons from wild-type and CMA-deficient (LAMP2A knockdown) mice, we will perform compartment-specific manipulations. We will quantify CMA flux using a validated fluorescent reporter (KFERQ-PA-mCherry-1) and assess the localization of CMA components (LAMP2A, HSC70) via immunofluorescence and live imaging. Pharmacological CMA activators (e.g., AR7, a caloric restriction mimetic) and stressors will be applied to specific compartments to probe regulatory mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, high-resolution map of CMA activity in neuronal subdomains, defining its role in maintaining axonal and synaptic proteostasis. It will establish a foundational platform to test how CMA-targeted therapies, like caloric restriction mimetics, act within specific neuronal circuits, bridging the gap between systemic CMA modulation and precise neuronal repair.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics have been used for general autophagy studies, applying this technology to dissect the compartment-specific mechanics of *chaperone-mediated* autophagy, a selective and mechanistically distinct pathway, in healthy and diseased neurons is a novel and critical advance.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal mapping of APP processing by the autophagy-lysosome system using in situ proximity ligation assay
proposals.motivation: Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathogenesis is driven by amyloidogenic processing of APP by β- and γ-secretases, generating neurotoxic Aβ peptides. Pre-2026 work, including from the Cuervo lab, shows that chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) and other lysosomal pathways can degrade APP and secretases, but the spatial and temporal dynamics of these interactions in intact cells remain poorly resolved. Understanding this competition between processing and degradation is critical for developing therapies that shift the balance toward clearance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that APP and its secretases (BACE1 and presenilin-1) engage in transient, spatially restricted interactions with core CMA components (LAMP2A, HSC70) and macroautophagy proteins (LC3), and that these interactions are dysregulated in cellular models of AD pathology, favoring amyloidogenesis over clearance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Duolink® Proximity Ligation Assay (PLA) for fixed cells and brain sections. Primary antibodies against APP, BACE1, presenilin-1, LAMP2A, HSC70, and LC3 will be used in pairwise combinations with species-specific PLA probes. Signal amplification and fluorescent detection will allow visualization of protein complexes (<40 nm apart) in situ. We will quantify PLA foci in neuronal cell lines under basal conditions, CMA activation (via 6-aminonicotinamide or serum starvation), and CMA inhibition (LAMP2A knockdown), and in hippocampal sections from APP/PS1 transgenic mice versus age-matched wild-types.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, visual evidence of close-proximity interactions between the APP processing machinery and autophagic components in a native cellular context. It will define the subcellular compartments where these interactions occur and quantify how they change with CMA modulation and AD progression. The findings will establish a foundational spatial map for targeting specific protein interactions to enhance APP clearance.
proposals.novelty: While PLA has been used to study APP secretase interactions or autophagy separately, its application to directly visualize the interface between APP processing and autophagic degradation pathways in situ is novel. This approach moves beyond co-immunoprecipitation and colocalization studies by proving physical proximity, offering unprecedented spatial resolution of these critical competitive interactions.

proposals.title: Reconstitution of Amyloid-β Generation in a Cell-Free System Using Purified Single Autophagosomes
proposals.motivation: The autophagosome has been implicated as a key site for the generation of amyloid-β (Aβ) peptides in Alzheimer's disease, but the precise molecular mechanisms and regulation within this compartment remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 work, including studies on chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) and noncanonical autophagy functions, highlights the complex role of autophagy in protein homeostasis and disease, yet a direct, isolated system to dissect Aβ biogenesis is lacking. This gap limits our ability to identify specific therapeutic targets within the autophagic pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that purified, single autophagosomes contain the necessary enzymatic machinery (e.g., γ-secretase) and substrate (APP-CTF) to generate Aβ peptides, and that this process can be modulated by CMA components and metabolic regulators like those activated by calorie restriction.
proposals.method: We will establish a cell-free reconstitution assay using single autophagosomes purified via immunoisolation from neuronal cell lines or primary neurons, leveraging techniques for organelle purification refined in the Cuervo lab. The purified autophagosomes will be incubated in a controlled buffer system, and Aβ generation will be quantified over time using sensitive ELISA and mass spectrometry. We will pharmacologically and genetically manipulate the system by pre-treating donor cells with CMA activators (e.g., AR7) or inhibitors, and by supplementing the assay with purified chaperones (e.g., Hsc70) and LAMP2A to test their direct role in modulating Aβ production within the autophagosome.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence and quantitative analysis of Aβ generation within a defined autophagosomal compartment, establishing a foundational *in vitro* system. It will delineate the specific contribution of autophagosomes versus other organelles to Aβ pathology and identify how CMA, a pathway known to decline with age, regulates this process, offering new mechanistic insights into Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis.
proposals.novelty: While the link between autophagy and Aβ is known, a reductionist, single-organelle, cell-free reconstitution system to study this event is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond correlative cellular studies to establish direct causality and enables the precise dissection of molecular players within the autophagosome itself.

proposals.title: Chaperone-mediated autophagy dysfunction as a convergent node in tauopathy and APOE4-driven Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 Alzheimer's disease (AD) research has heavily focused on amyloid-β (Aβ) pathology, often using APP/PS1 models, but clinical trials targeting Aβ have shown limited success. This underscores the need to investigate pathogenic mechanisms in non-Aβ-centric models, particularly those driven by tauopathy and the major genetic risk factor APOE4, which are critical for late-onset AD. The role of autophagy, specifically chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), in clearing pathogenic proteins like tau and its dysfunction in APOE4 contexts remains poorly defined, representing a significant knowledge gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA activity is compromised in tauopathy and APOE4-based AD models, and that selective restoration of CMA, particularly via LAMP2A, will reduce tau aggregation and associated neuronal dysfunction, independent of Aβ pathology.
proposals.method: We will employ primary neuronal cultures and brain tissue from established pre-2026 mouse models: the PS19 tauopathy model and targeted replacement APOE4 mice. CMA activity will be quantified using established assays measuring lysosomal uptake and degradation of the CMA substrate GAPDH. We will use viral vectors (AAV) for neuron-specific overexpression of LAMP2A to restore CMA. Outcomes will be assessed via biochemical analysis (sarkosyl-insoluble tau, phospho-tau blots), immunohistochemistry, and functional assays including synaptic protein analysis and in vivo microdialysis for neuronal metabolic stress. The role of CMA in Treg cell function, given Dr. Cuervo's recent findings, will be examined in APOE4 mice by flow cytometry of T cell compartments post-CMA restoration.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish CMA's role in tau and APOE4-driven AD pathogenesis, moving beyond the Aβ-centric paradigm. It will identify CMA as a potential convergent therapeutic target for diverse AD subtypes and provide mechanistic insight into how protein clearance failure contributes to disease, linking proteostasis to immune dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While CMA's role in APP/PS1 models is known, its function in pure tauopathy and APOE4 models is unexplored. Testing CMA restoration as a strategy in these non-Aβ models and investigating its impact on the recently identified T cell-AD axis represents a novel, integrative approach.

proposals.title: Identification of Nutrient-Sensing Kinase Pathways that Initiate Selective Autophagic Degradation of the NCoR1 Corepressor
proposals.motivation: Fasting triggers profound metabolic reprogramming, in part through the selective autophagic degradation of the nuclear receptor corepressor 1 (NCoR1), a key transcriptional brake on catabolic genes. While the functional outcome of NCoR1 loss is known, the specific fasting-induced signals and upstream kinases that initiate its recognition and degradation via autophagy remain undefined. Elucidating this pathway is critical for understanding how nutrient status is transduced into a precise autophagic response to regulate metabolism, with implications for aging and metabolic diseases where autophagy is dysregulated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fasting activates specific nutrient-sensing kinases (e.g., AMPK, ULK1) which directly phosphorylate NCoR1, creating a phospho-degron that is recognized by selective autophagy receptors, thereby targeting NCoR1 for lysosomal degradation.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of phospho-proteomics in liver tissue from fasted vs. fed mice to map fasting-induced phosphorylation sites on NCoR1. Candidate kinases will be validated using genetic (siRNA/shRNA) and pharmacological inhibition in primary hepatocytes, followed by monitoring of NCoR1 degradation via immunoblot and immunofluorescence. We will use co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays to identify the specific autophagy receptor (e.g., p62, NBR1) that binds phosphorylated NCoR1. Finally, we will generate phospho-deficient and phospho-mimetic NCoR1 mutants to test their stability and functional impact on fasting-responsive gene expression in vivo using adenoviral delivery.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel signaling axis linking nutrient deprivation to transcriptional reprogramming via selective autophagy. It will identify the specific kinase 'trigger' and receptor mechanism for NCoR1 degradation, adding a key regulatory node to the map of metabolic autophagy. The findings could reveal new targets for modulating autophagy-specific substrates, relevant for calorie restriction mimetics and immune-metabolic interventions.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Cuervo's lab has established chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) activation by fasting and the role of SKP1 in autophagy decisions, the upstream signaling that designates a specific nuclear protein like NCoR1 as an autophagic cargo during a physiological stimulus is unknown. This proposal moves beyond cataloging degraded proteins to mechanistically dissecting the initiating signal.

proposals.title: Functional Heterogeneity of Autophagic Vacuoles: Differential Roles of Autophagosomes, Multilamellar Bodies, and Autophagolysosomes in Proteotoxicity and Cellular Protection
proposals.motivation: Autophagic vacuoles (AVs) are traditionally viewed as a continuum leading to degradation, but distinct subtypes like autophagosomes, multilamellar bodies, and autophagolysosomes may have unique, non-equivalent functions. In aging and neurodegenerative diseases, the accumulation of specific AV subtypes is a hallmark, yet it remains unclear whether some are protective compartments for sequestering toxic material or are themselves pathogenic entities that disrupt cellular homeostasis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct autophagic vacuole subtypes have non-equivalent functional impacts, with specific subtypes (e.g., autophagolysosomes) being primarily protective, while others (e.g., certain multilamellar bodies) may contribute to cellular toxicity when their maturation or clearance is impaired.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of high-content imaging and correlative light-electron microscopy in live cells to track the formation and fate of individual AV subtypes labeled with specific markers (e.g., LC3, LAMP1, LAMP2A). Using biosensors for proteotoxicity and compartment-specific pH, we will functionally characterize each subtype. We will then apply selective pharmacological and genetic tools (e.g., SKP1 modulation to bias autophagy vs. secretion, CMA activation via CR mimetics) to manipulate the abundance of specific AVs and assess their impact on cell viability and protein aggregate clearance in models of proteostatic stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond a linear view of autophagy by establishing a functional map of AV subtypes. It will identify which compartments are cytoprotective and which may be biomarkers or drivers of pathology, providing a refined framework for understanding autophagy failure in disease. The findings could inform more targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at modulating specific AV dynamics rather than bulk autophagy.
proposals.novelty: While AVs are known to accumulate, their individual functional capacities are treated as uniform. This proposal directly challenges the assumption of functional equivalence by applying Dr. Cuervo's expertise in compartment-specific autophagy (CMA) and unconventional secretion to dissect the unique roles of macro-autophagic subtypes, a question not systematically addressed pre-2026.

proposals.title: Dysregulation of Autophagic Flux in Alzheimer's Disease Neurons: A Quantitative Analysis of Induction versus Clearance
proposals.motivation: Autophagic vacuoles accumulate in Alzheimer's disease (AD) neurons, but it is unclear if this reflects hyper-induction of autophagy or a failure in lysosomal clearance. This distinction is critical for therapeutic targeting, as stimulating a blocked pathway could be harmful. Pre-2026 work by the Cuervo lab has established the importance of chaperone-mediated autophagy and lysosomal function in aging and neurodegeneration, but a precise quantification of autophagic flux in human AD tissue is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the accumulation of autophagosomes in AD neurons is primarily due to impaired lysosomal clearance (degradation) rather than an increase in autophagosome formation (induction).
proposals.method: We will quantify autophagic flux in post-mortem human prefrontal cortex samples from AD and age-matched control brains using a validated tandem-fluorescence mRFP-GFP-LC3 reporter system delivered via adeno-associated virus (AAV) to ex vivo brain slice cultures. The ratio of red-only puncta (autolysosomes) to yellow puncta (autophagosomes) provides a direct measure of flux. This will be complemented by immunoblotting for LC3-II and p62/SQSTM1 in the presence and absence of lysosomal inhibitors (bafilomycin A1) to calculate flux rates, and correlated with markers of lysosomal function (LAMP2A, cathepsin activity) and AD pathology (phospho-tau, Aβ).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative measurement of autophagic flux in human AD neurons, definitively identifying the defective step. It will bridge the gap between pathological observations and mechanistic understanding, directly informing whether therapies should aim to enhance lysosomal degradation or modulate autophagic induction. The findings will integrate with Dr. Cuervo's expertise in chaperone-mediated autophagy and lysosomal rejuvenation.
proposals.novelty: While autophagic accumulation is a known feature of AD, prior studies have been largely qualitative or based on static markers. Applying a dynamic flux assay to human ex vivo tissue, combined with lysosomal functional assessment, offers a novel and direct test of the prevailing but unproven assumption of impaired clearance.

proposals.title: Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Failure Drives Synaptic Loss via Autophagic Vacuole Accumulation in Alzheimer's Disease Models
proposals.motivation: Synaptic failure is the strongest pathological correlate of cognitive decline in Alzheimer's disease (AD). Pre-2026 work has shown massive accumulation of autophagic vacuoles (AVs) in dystrophic neurites of AD brains, but whether this is a cause or a consequence of synaptic dysfunction is unknown. Given Dr. Cuervo's seminal work linking chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) decline to aging and neurodegeneration, we hypothesize that CMA failure at the synapse is a primary driver of AV accumulation and subsequent synapse loss.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that selective impairment of chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) in neurons, particularly at synaptic terminals, leads to the pathological accumulation of autophagic vacuoles, which directly disrupts synaptic vesicle recycling and triggers synaptic degeneration in AD.
proposals.method: We will use adeno-associated viruses (AAVs) to express a synaptic-targeted LAMP2A mutant (to rescue CMA) or a dominant-negative LAMP2A construct (to inhibit CMA) in hippocampal neurons of 5xFAD mice. Using super-resolution microscopy (STORM) and array tomography, we will quantify the co-localization of AV markers (LC3, p62) with synaptic proteins (synaptophysin, PSD-95) and CMA substrates (MEF2D, RHEB). We will employ in vivo 2-photon imaging through cranial windows to longitudinally track synaptic puncta and AV accumulation in the same animals. Synaptic function will be assessed via electrophysiology (fEPSPs) and a novel fluorescent reporter of synaptic vesicle endocytosis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal link between a specific autophagic pathway (CMA), AV accumulation, and synapse loss, moving beyond correlative observations. It will identify CMA components at the synapse as novel therapeutic targets for preserving synaptic integrity in AD. The project will also deliver new methodologies for in vivo, longitudinal imaging of autophagy and synaptic dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While AV accumulation in AD neurites is known, this proposal uniquely posits CMA failure as the upstream cause and tests its direct, mechanistic role in synaptic dysfunction using Dr. Cuervo's specialized tools for manipulating CMA in a cell- and compartment-specific manner, which has not been done in the synaptic context.

proposals.title: CLEM-based ultrastructural mapping of chaperone-mediated autophagy dynamics in aging neurons
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is crucial for neuronal proteostasis, and its decline is a hallmark of aging and neurodegenerative diseases. However, the dynamic spatiotemporal relationship between CMA initiation, substrate targeting, and lysosomal fusion in live neurons remains poorly defined at the ultrastructural level, limiting our understanding of its functional failure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA substrates in neurons are selectively sequestered into distinct, transient pre-lysosomal compartments whose ultrastructure and molecular composition change predictably during aging, and that these changes can be reversed by CMA activators like caloric restriction mimetics.
proposals.method: We will use live-cell confocal microscopy in primary cortical neurons from young and old mice to track GFP-tagged LAMP2A and mCherry-tagged KFERQ-substrate reporters. Following time-lapse imaging of CMA flux under basal and stimulated conditions, cells will be immediately fixed and processed for correlative electron microscopy (CLEM) using fiducial markers. The same fluorescent structures will be located via integrated light-electron microscopy (iLEM) or software-based correlation (e.g., MAPS software from Thermo Fisher) for subsequent ultrastructural analysis of their membrane morphology, content, and proximity to organelles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first ultrastructural roadmap of functional CMA compartments in neurons, directly linking dynamic live-cell CMA activity to nanoscale morphology. It will identify specific structural correlates of CMA efficiency and failure in aging, offering new biomarkers and structural endpoints for evaluating CMA-targeted therapies.
proposals.novelty: While CLEM has been applied to macroautophagy, its use to resolve the elusive, non-vesicular stages of CMA in neurons is novel. Combining this with aging models and CMA modulators allows us to directly test how age-related changes in T cell function, recently linked to LAMP2A, correlate with neuronal CMA ultrastructure.

proposals.title: Dysregulation of Single Autophagosome Biogenesis Dynamics in Alzheimer's Disease Neurons
proposals.motivation: Impaired macroautophagy is a hallmark of Alzheimer's disease (AD), contributing to toxic protein aggregate accumulation. However, pre-2026 studies primarily assess bulk autophagic flux, obscuring potential defects in the initiation, expansion, or closure of individual autophagic vesicles (AVs). High-resolution, single-event analysis is needed to pinpoint the precise biogenesis failure in AD neurons.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AD neurons exhibit specific kinetic defects in the nucleation, expansion, or scission of individual autophagosomes, driven by compromised chaperone and regulatory protein function at nascent phagophore sites.
proposals.method: We will employ live-cell, high-temporal-resolution (seconds) spinning-disk confocal imaging of primary hippocampal neurons from AD mouse models (e.g., 5xFAD) expressing fluorescently tagged autophagy markers (e.g., LC3, ATG5). Using custom particle tracking and event analysis algorithms (based on TrackMate and custom Python scripts for kinetic profiling), we will quantify the duration and progression of single AV biogenesis events. This will be combined with pharmacological (e.g., caloric restriction mimetics) and genetic (e.g., LAMP2A overexpression) modulation of chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) to test its role in regulating macroautophagy initiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first kinetic map of single autophagosome biogenesis in healthy versus AD neurons, identifying the exact stage of dysfunction. It will establish a causal link between CMA activity (a key Cuervo lab theme) and the efficiency of macroautophagy nucleation, revealing a novel regulatory axis for therapeutic targeting in proteostatic diseases.
proposals.novelty: While bulk autophagy is known to be impaired in AD, the direct observation and kinetic dissection of single AV formation in diseased neurons is unexplored. Linking CMA status to the dynamics of macroautophagy initiation at the single-event level represents a novel, non-canonical cross-talk between proteolytic pathways.

proposals.title: Dynamic Regulation of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy: Identifying Inducers and Stress-Specific Substrates
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway critical for protein quality control and cellular metabolism, whose activity declines with age and in neurodegenerative diseases. It is widely described as a constitutive pathway, but this assumption limits our understanding of its physiological regulation and therapeutic potential. Determining if and how CMA can be dynamically upregulated beyond basal levels is essential for designing strategies to boost CMA in pathological conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA is not merely constitutive but can be specifically induced by distinct cellular stressors, leading to the recruitment of a unique repertoire of protein substrates tailored to each stress condition.
proposals.method: We will use a combination of established CMA reporter cell lines (KFERQ-PA-mCherry-1) and novel biosensors to monitor real-time CMA flux under diverse stresses (oxidative, proteotoxic, metabolic). Quantitative proteomics of lysosome-enriched fractions (LAMP2A co-immunoprecipitation coupled with mass spectrometry) will identify stress-specific CMA substrates. We will employ CRISPRi/a screens targeting known CMA components (LAMP2A, HSC70) and regulators of related pathways (like SKP1, implicated in CMA-secretion switching) to uncover novel inducers.
proposals.contribution: This work will redefine CMA from a static housekeeping process to a dynamically regulated protective response, identifying novel physiological inducers. It will provide a map of stress-specific CMA substrates, offering mechanistic insight into how selective autophagy tailors its response to maintain proteostasis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work from Cuervo's lab shows CMA can be activated by interventions like calorie restriction, the field lacks a systematic investigation of endogenous, acute stress-induced CMA. Challenging the dogma of constitutive-only activity and linking specific inducers to unique substrate profiles is a fundamentally new approach.

proposals.title: Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy as a Regulator of Protein Function: Investigating Incomplete Degradation and Substrate Release from LAMP2A
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway critical for cellular proteostasis, metabolism, and immune function, as shown by its decline in aging and its activation by calorie restriction. The canonical model posits that KFERQ-tagged substrates, upon binding to the lysosomal receptor LAMP2A, are fully unfolded and translocated into the lumen for complete degradation. However, the functional consequences of this binary model may be oversimplified, potentially overlooking regulatory roles for partial processing or release of intact proteins, which could expand CMA's role in cell signaling beyond mere clearance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of proteins bound to the CMA translocation complex at the lysosomal membrane are not fully degraded but are instead partially processed or released intact back into the cytosol, representing a non-canonical, regulatory function of CMA.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of established CMA assays and novel reporters. Using lysosomes isolated from mouse liver or cultured cells, we will perform in vitro CMA assays with well-characterized substrates (e.g., GAPDH, RNase A). We will develop a novel, cleavage-sensitive fluorescent reporter protein containing a CMA-targeting motif and a shielded fluorophore that is only exposed upon partial proteolysis. This, combined with pulse-chase experiments, immunoblotting for substrate fragments, and co-immunoprecipitation of LAMP2A complexes under conditions that stall translocation (e.g., ATP depletion, specific inhibitors), will allow us to detect and quantify partially processed or released full-length substrates.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the prevailing 'all-or-nothing' paradigm of CMA by providing the first direct evidence for incomplete substrate processing. It will establish a potential new mechanism by which CMA can modulate protein activity and cellular signaling networks, beyond its known catabolic role. This could explain rapid, CMA-dependent regulatory changes observed in metabolism and immune function (e.g., in T cells) that are difficult to reconcile solely with changes in protein abundance.
proposals.novelty: While CMA's role in degradation is well-defined, the possibility of regulated substrate release or partial processing is unexplored. This proposal is novel in directly testing whether the CMA machinery can function as a conditional processing or release platform, akin to non-canonical roles recently identified in other autophagy pathways (e.g., SKP1's role in switching autophagy to secretion).

proposals.title: Stress-Induced Remodeling of the LGP96 Cytosolic Tail Switches Its Substrate Interactome to Direct Adaptive Cellular Responses
proposals.motivation: Lysosomal membrane proteins like LAMP2A are critical for chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), a selective degradation pathway essential for cellular proteostasis and immune function, as shown by Dr. Cuervo's work. However, the role of other lysosomal receptors, such as LGP96 (Lysosomal Glycoprotein 96), in stress adaptation remains poorly defined. Understanding how cellular stress dynamically reprograms the substrate selectivity of lysosomal receptors could reveal novel regulatory nodes for maintaining cellular health and combating age-related diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific cellular stresses induce post-translational modifications (PTMs) or co-factor interactions on the cytosolic tail of LGP96, functionally remodeling its substrate recognition motif and thereby recruiting a distinct repertoire of signaling proteins to direct condition-specific adaptive responses.
proposals.method: We will first define the baseline LGP96 substrate-binding motif using peptide phage display and co-immunoprecipitation/mass spectrometry (co-IP/MS) in unstressed cells. We will then subject cells to defined stresses (e.g., ER stress with tunicamycin, nutrient deprivation, viral mimic poly(I:C)) and analyze stress-induced changes via PTM-specific proteomics, site-directed mutagenesis, and functional CMA/reporter assays.
