The role of specific interacting co-factors, including chaperones like Hsc70, will be assessed using siRNA knockdowns and proximity ligation assays to map the dynamic interactome.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish LGP96 as a dynamic, stress-responsive signaling node at the lysosome, moving beyond a static receptor model. It will provide a mechanistic map of how environmental cues rewire lysosomal receptor connectivity, potentially identifying new regulatory interfaces for modulating proteostasis and immune function in aging and disease.
proposals.novelty: While LAMP2A's role in CMA is established, the concept that stress actively reprograms the substrate-binding interface of a lysosomal receptor like LGP96 to switch its signaling output is novel. This integrates structural motif analysis with dynamic cellular signaling in a way not previously applied to this protein family.

proposals.title: Regulation of the Lysosomal GP96 (LGP96) Receptor in Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy: Dynamics, Stress Response, and Functional Impact on Immune Cell Proteostasis
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a critical proteolytic pathway for selective lysosomal degradation of cytosolic proteins, essential for cellular homeostasis, stress adaptation, and immune function. While the LAMP2A receptor is the established limiting component of CMA, recent pre-2026 work from the Cuervo lab identified a novel lysosomal GP96 (LGP96) protein that functions as a receptor for a subset of CMA substrates, suggesting a more complex regulatory landscape. How LGP96 expression and activity are dynamically controlled, and how this regulation integrates with LAMP2A under different physiological and stress conditions, remains completely unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LGP96 expression and receptor complex assembly are regulated distinctly from LAMP2A, responding to specific cellular stresses and metabolic cues, and that this differential regulation tailors CMA substrate selectivity, with particular relevance for proteostasis in immune cells like T cells.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of cellular and molecular approaches. First, we will generate stable cell lines with fluorescently tagged LGP96 and LAMP2A to monitor their real-time trafficking and lysosomal levels via live-cell imaging and quantitative immunofluorescence under basal conditions, nutrient deprivation (to activate CMA), oxidative stress, and proteotoxic stress. Second, we will use co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays to define the dynamics of LGP96's interaction with the CMA translocation complex (Hsc70, lysosomal Hsc70) compared to LAMP2A. Third, leveraging our expertise in T cell models, we will analyze LGP96 expression and CMA flux in young versus old mouse T cells, and in Tregs versus effector T cells, using flow cytometry, immunoblotting, and a validated CMA reporter assay.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of LGP96 regulation, establishing it as a dynamically controlled node in the CMA pathway. It will elucidate how cells fine-tune substrate selectivity through differential receptor regulation. Furthermore, by linking LGP96 dynamics to immune cell function, it may reveal novel mechanisms underlying age-related immune decline and offer new targets for immunomodulation.
proposals.novelty: While LAMP2A regulation is well-studied, the control of the newly identified LGP96 receptor is entirely unexplored. This proposal moves beyond the canonical single-receptor model of CMA to investigate how a multi-receptor system is coordinated, a non-obvious layer of complexity in selective autophagy.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Kinetic Profiling of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Reveals Regulatory Nodes and Alternative Substrate Fates in Live Cells
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway critical for cellular proteostasis, metabolism, and aging. While the stepwise process is known, the dynamic regulation, kinetic heterogeneity, and alternative fates (e.g., substrate recycling, abortive disassembly) of CMA components in living cells remain unquantified. This gap limits our understanding of how CMA adapts to metabolic stress or aging, as seen in immune dysfunction and disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CMA pathway operates with significant kinetic heterogeneity and contains key regulatory nodes whose optogenetic perturbation can shift substrate flux between degradation, recycling, and abortive pathways, revealing the system's adaptive logic.
proposals.method: We will employ single-molecule tracking of HSC70 and LAMP2A, combined with multiplexed fluorescence biosensors for substrate binding, translocation, and lysosomal proteolysis in live cells. Computational particle tracking and Bayesian inference algorithms will be used to derive kinetic rates. Concurrently, we will use optogenetic tools (e.g., Cry2/CIB systems) to dynamically cluster or sequester key components like LAMP2A or HSC70, testing how acute perturbations alter the measured kinetic parameters under basal, starved, and oxidative stress conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative, predictive kinetic model of the complete CMA cycle in living cells. It will identify the rate-limiting steps and regulatory nodes that control CMA flux, and define non-canonical substrate fates. This model will provide a framework to understand CMA dysregulation in aging and disease, such as in T cell function, and to evaluate the mechanistic impact of calorie restriction mimetics.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond static, endpoint assays to quantify the real-time kinetics and decision points of CMA at the single-molecule level in live cells is novel. The integration of optogenetic perturbations with single-molecule kinetics to map the system's regulatory logic represents a significant advance over existing correlative studies.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Kinetic Mapping of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Substrate Translocation and Degradation
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a critical lysosomal degradation pathway for selective proteins, whose dysfunction is linked to aging, neurodegeneration, and metabolic disease. While the key components (Hsc70, LAMP-2A) are known, the complete sequence of molecular events—from substrate recognition to proteolysis—remains a kinetic and mechanistic 'black box'. This lack of a detailed, stepwise map hinders our ability to identify precise points of failure in pathology and to design targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CMA pathway comprises discrete, quantifiable steps of substrate recognition, unfolding, translocation, and degradation, and that applying single-molecule biophysics will reveal the kinetic bottlenecks and mechanical forces governing this process.
proposals.method: We will adapt single-molecule fluorescence (TIRF microscopy, FRET) and force spectroscopy (optical tweezers) techniques, analogous to those used for nuclear pore transport. A model CMA substrate (e.g., GAPDH or RNase A) will be site-specifically labeled with fluorophores or attached to microspheres. In reconstituted systems using purified components (Hsc70, lysosomal membranes) and in permeabilized cells, we will track in real-time: 1) binding kinetics to the lysosomal receptor complex, 2) conformational changes during ATP-dependent unfolding using FRET, 3) dwell times during LAMP-2A pore translocation, and 4) final handoff and cleavage by intraluminal cathepsins.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, kinetic map of a complete lysosomal degradation pathway, defining the rate-limiting steps in CMA. It will directly measure the mechanical work of chaperone-driven unfolding and translocation, offering quantitative principles for how selective autophagy pathways operate. This foundational knowledge will provide a new framework for understanding CMA decline in aging and disease.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the rigorous, step-resolution toolkit of single-molecule biophysics, developed for systems like nuclear transport, to the entirely unexplored mechanistic landscape of lysosomal substrate import, moving beyond static biochemical assays to dynamic, real-time observation of the entire degradation trajectory.

proposals.title: A Dominant-Negative LGP96 Mutant to Dissect the Role of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy in T Cell Function and Immune Aging
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway crucial for cellular proteostasis, metabolism, and immune function, with its activity declining with age. Pre-2026 work from the Cuervo lab has shown that restoring CMA via LAMP2A expression in old mice improves T cell function, but the specific mechanistic contributions of CMA to T cell biology and the consequences of its acute, cell-type-specific inhibition remain poorly understood. A tool to selectively block CMA in defined cell populations is needed to establish causal links between CMA loss and age-related immune dysfunction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering a dominant-negative mutant of the key CMA receptor LGP96 (Lysosome-associated membrane protein type 2A, LAMP2A) will allow for cell-specific inhibition of CMA, and that loss of CMA function in T cells will recapitulate age-like phenotypes, including impaired Treg function and altered metabolic profiles.
proposals.method: We will design a non-functional LGP96 mutant by introducing point mutations (e.g., in the substrate-binding domain or transmembrane region) based on pre-2026 structural and functional knowledge. The mutant will be cloned into lentiviral vectors for inducible expression in primary T cells and T cell lines. We will validate dominant-negative function by assessing binding to CMA substrates (HSC70), lysosomal translocation, and degradation rates compared to wild-type LGP96. The mutant will then be selectively expressed in Tregs and effector T cells using Cre-lox systems in vitro and in vivo to study loss-of-function phenotypes on immune signaling, metabolism, and function in young and aged contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first genetically encoded, inducible tool for cell-specific inhibition of CMA, moving beyond global knockouts or pharmacological approaches. It will directly test the causal role of CMA in maintaining T cell compartment integrity and function, offering mechanistic insight into how CMA decline contributes to immunosenescence. The findings could validate CMA as a therapeutic target for age-related immune decline.
proposals.novelty: While CMA activation has been studied, a dominant-negative approach for its specific, acute inhibition in defined cell types is novel. Applying this tool to dissect CMA's role in T cell subsets, particularly Tregs, integrates the lab's expertise in CMA with recent findings on immune aging in a unique, mechanistically focused way.

proposals.title: Systemic analysis of LGP96 reveals its role in organismal proteostasis and immune function via selective autophagy
proposals.motivation: Selective autophagy pathways are critical for cellular quality control, but their systemic physiological roles in whole organisms remain poorly understood. LGP96 (Lysosomal Glycoprotein 96) is a putative selective autophagy receptor identified in pre-2026 proteomic studies, yet its in vivo function is unknown. Investigating LGP96 is crucial to understand how selective degradation integrates with systemic processes like aging and immunity, areas central to Dr. Cuervo's research on chaperone-mediated autophagy and immune restoration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LGP96 functions as a key selective autophagy receptor in vivo, and its genetic deletion will lead to systemic proteostatic failure, metabolic dysregulation, and impaired immune cell function, particularly in T cell compartments.
proposals.method: We will generate a constitutive LGP96 knockout mouse model using CRISPR-Cas9. A comprehensive phenotypic analysis will be performed, including assessment of lifespan, metabolic parameters, and tissue-specific proteostasis via ubiquitinated protein aggregates and p62/SQSTM1 levels. Immune function will be evaluated by flow cytometry of T cell subsets (naïve, effector, memory, Tregs) from spleen and lymph nodes, and by response to immune challenge. We will employ co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays to identify LGP96's cargo proteins and interactors within the autophagy machinery.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the first in vivo physiological role for LGP96, establishing it as a novel player in organismal proteostasis. It will directly link a specific selective autophagy pathway to systemic immune function, providing a mechanistic basis for how proteostatic decline contributes to immunosenescence. The findings may identify LGP96 as a potential therapeutic target for age-related diseases.
proposals.novelty: While prior work from the Cuervo lab restored LAMP2A to improve immune function, this proposal investigates a novel, uncharacterized receptor (LGP96) from the ground up. It moves beyond correlative studies to establish direct causal links between a specific selective autophagy pathway and systemic physiology in a whole organism, a level of analysis rarely achieved for newly identified autophagy components.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Role of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy in Age-Independent Pathogenesis of Autoimmune and Metabolic Disease
proposals.motivation: The hypothesis that aging is the primary driver of all chronic disease is compelling but may obscure distinct, early-life pathogenic mechanisms. Pre-2026 work by Cuervo and others shows chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a key proteostatic pathway that declines with age, but its selective role in specific immune and metabolic cell functions suggests it could also be a primary, age-independent disease node. This is critical for identifying true causal drivers versus age-associated correlates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic or acute functional impairment of CMA in specific cell types (e.g., Tregs, hepatocytes) is sufficient to initiate chronic disease phenotypes independently of chronological aging, while other diseases are primarily driven by age-related CMA decline.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-disease mouse model approach. Using conditional LAMP2A knockout mice (pre-2026 established models), we will acutely deplete CMA in adult Tregs (Foxp3-CreERT2) to model autoimmunity and in hepatocytes (Alb-CreERT2) to model metabolic dysfunction. Disease progression (autoantibodies, glucose tolerance) will be tracked in young (3-6 month) and middle-aged (12-14 month) cohorts. We will integrate this with a 'selective CMA restoration' strategy using viral vectors in aged mice, comparing disease reversal in autoimmune (e.g., SLE-prone) versus metabolic (high-fat diet) models to parse age-dependent from -independent effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the primacy of aging in chronic disease etiology by mechanistically separating CMA's role as an initiating factor from its role as a declining maintenance pathway. It will provide a functional map of which diseases are truly 'driven by aging' versus those with age-independent CMA-dependent triggers, refining therapeutic targets for prevention versus reversal.
proposals.novelty: While CMA's decline in aging is known, its sufficiency as a primary, age-independent driver of specific chronic diseases has not been systematically tested. The approach leverages Cuervo's unique tools for selective CMA manipulation to challenge the monolithic view of aging as the root cause.

proposals.title: Primary lysosomal acidification failure as a driver of chaperone-mediated autophagy dysfunction in PS1-deficient models
proposals.motivation: Presenilin-1 (PS1) loss-of-function is linked to Alzheimer's disease and cellular aging, impairing chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA). While lysosomal acidification defects are observed, it is unclear if this is a direct consequence of PS1 loss or a secondary adaptation to other stresses like proteostatic imbalance. Determining causality is critical for designing therapies targeting lysosomal function versus upstream pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PS1 deficiency directly impairs the activity and/or assembly of the V-ATPase proton pump, leading to a primary defect in lysosomal acidification that subsequently drives CMA failure and proteotoxicity.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR/Cas9 to generate PS1 knockout cell lines and validate with PS1 conditional knockout mice. Lysosomal pH will be measured in real-time using ratiometric LysoSensor probes and compared to isogenic controls. To test causality, we will acutely rescue acidification using pharmacological V-ATPase activators (e.g., EN6) or via targeted expression of the V1 domain subunit ATP6V1A, and assess restoration of CMA flux using the KFERQ-PA-mCherry reporter and levels of LAMP2A. We will also examine V-ATPase assembly via co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays in PS1-deficient lysosomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a mechanistic hierarchy between PS1 function and lysosomal acidification, clarifying a key early event in CMA decline. It will identify whether V-ATPase is a direct therapeutic target for restoring proteostasis in PS1-related pathologies.
proposals.novelty: This directly tests the causality of the acidification defect, moving beyond correlation, by using acute, specific rescue of pH independent of PS1 restoration, an approach not yet applied in this context.

proposals.title: The PS1-v-ATPase Axis Regulates Lysosomal pH and Unconventional Secretion in Professional Secretory Cells
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Ana Maria Cuervo's lab established that Presenilin 1 (PS1) regulates lysosomal acidification via the v-ATPase V0a1 subunit, impacting autophagy and protein degradation, primarily in neurons. However, the role of this mechanism in non-neuronal cells with high secretory and lysosomal demand, such as pancreatic acinar cells or plasma cells, is unknown. Given the lab's recent findings on the SKP1-mediated switch between autophagy and unconventional secretion, understanding how PS1-v-ATPase activity influences secretory pathways in these cells could reveal fundamental cell biology with implications for exocrine disorders and antibody secretion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the PS1-v-ATPase mechanism is critical for maintaining lysosomal pH and function in professional secretory cells, and that its dysregulation shifts the balance from degradative autophagy to unconventional secretion, thereby impacting cellular proteostasis and secretory output.
proposals.method: We will use primary pancreatic acinar cells and a plasma cell line as models. PS1 will be knocked down or inhibited (using DAPT), and lysosomal pH will be measured ratiometrically using LysoSensor Yellow/Blue DND-160. v-ATPase assembly and activity will be assessed by co-immunoprecipitation of PS1 with V0a1 and an in vitro ATPase assay. The flux through chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) will be monitored using the KFERQ-PA-mCherry reporter, while unconventional secretion will be tracked via secretion of ANXA1 (a known CUPS-dependent cargo) into the conditioned medium. Proteomic analysis of secreted vesicles will identify additional cargoes.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel, cell-type-specific function for the PS1-v-ATPase complex beyond neurons, linking lysosomal pH control to the secretory capacity of professional secretory cells. It will provide mechanistic insight into how metabolic sensors like the v-ATPase can dictate a cell's choice between degradation and secretion, expanding the understanding of proteostasis networks in health and disease.
proposals.novelty: While PS1's role in lysosomal pH is known in neurons, applying this mechanism to control the autophagy-secretion switch in professional secretory cells is a novel and non-obvious extension, directly building on the lab's unique expertise in chaperone-mediated autophagy and unconventional secretion.

proposals.title: Lysosomal pH as a Physiological Regulator of Presenilin-1 Endoproteolysis and γ-Secretase Complex Assembly
proposals.motivation: Presenilin-1 (PS1) endoproteolysis is essential for forming the active γ-secretase complex, a key protease in Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis. Lysosomal function, particularly chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), is known to decline with age and in neurodegenerative diseases, and lysosomal pH is a critical regulator of CMA activity. However, it is unknown whether the lysosomal lumen's pH environment directly influences the maturation or stability of PS1, representing a novel intersection between lysosomal health and amyloid precursor protein processing.
proposals.hypothesis: Chronic lysosomal alkalization, induced independently of PS1 function, will impair PS1 endoproteolysis, reduce its stability, and disrupt the assembly of the γ-secretase complex.
proposals.method: We will use established pharmacological (e.g., chloroquine, bafilomycin A1) and genetic (e.g., siRNA against the V0a1 subunit of the v-ATPase) tools to chronically alkalize lysosomes in HEK293 and neuronal cell lines. We will assess PS1 processing via immunoblotting for full-length and N- and C-terminal fragments, γ-secretase complex assembly via blue native PAGE and co-immunoprecipitation with nicastrin, and PS1 stability via cycloheximide chase assays. PS1 localization relative to alkalized lysosomes (marked by LysoTracker Red) will be analyzed by confocal microscopy. Functional readouts will include γ-secretase activity assays with recombinant C99 substrate.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between lysosomal pH and the proteolytic maturation of a key Alzheimer's disease-related protease. It will identify lysosomal alkalization as a novel cell biological mechanism potentially contributing to γ-secretase dysfunction beyond genetic mutations. The findings could inform the development of lysosome-targeting therapies aimed at restoring proper PS1 function.
proposals.novelty: While PS1 function influences lysosomal pH, the inverse regulation—where lysosomal pH controls PS1 biogenesis—has not been systematically tested. This proposal inverts the established causal direction to explore lysosomes as active signaling organelles regulating γ-secretase assembly, a concept not yet described in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Non-degradative Functions of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Components in T Cell Signaling and Immune Synapse Assembly
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is canonically defined as a selective lysosomal degradation pathway. However, recent pre-2026 work, including from our lab, shows that autophagy-related proteins can have non-canonical roles in secretion and signaling. Given our recent finding that CMA restoration in aged T cells improves immune function, we must test if CMA components like LAMP2A and HSC70 function beyond degradation to directly orchestrate immune cell communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core CMA components, particularly LAMP2A and HSC70, participate in non-degradative processes within T cells, such as facilitating signal transduction from the T cell receptor or nucleating protein complexes at the immune synapse.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach using primary murine Tregs and effector T cells. First, we will use proximity-dependent biotin identification (BioID) with LAMP2A and HSC70 as baits in activated T cells to map interactomes distinct from degradation substrates. Second, we will utilize super-resolution microscopy (STORM) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) to visualize and quantify the spatial organization and real-time dynamics of CMA components at the immune synapse during antigen presentation. Third, we will employ CRISPRi to selectively disrupt the lysosomal targeting of HSC70-LAMP2A complexes while preserving their cytosolic interactions, allowing functional dissection of degradative vs. non-degradative roles in T cell activation assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will redefine the functional scope of CMA, establishing a paradigm where its core machinery has dual degradative and non-degradative roles. It will provide a mechanistic explanation for how CMA modulates immune function beyond protein turnover, potentially identifying novel signaling hubs. The findings could open new avenues for therapeutic intervention in immune disorders by targeting CMA's assembly functions separately from its degradative activity.
proposals.novelty: While non-canonical roles for macroautophagy proteins are known, the exclusive degradative nature of CMA has never been challenged. This proposal directly tests the radical concept that the defining components of CMA have evolutionarily co-opted functions in complex assembly and signaling, a possibility not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Beyond KFERQ: Systematic Identification of Non-Canonical Motifs and Post-Translational Modifications that Confer Selectivity for Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a critical lysosomal degradation pathway for maintaining proteostasis, implicated in aging, neurodegeneration, and immune function. The canonical model posits that the KFERQ-like pentapeptide motif is the sole and necessary targeting signal for CMA. However, the proteome-wide specificity of CMA and its regulation in different physiological contexts, such as immune cell function and calorie restriction, may be more complex than this single motif can explain, suggesting the existence of additional targeting mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA substrate selectivity is not exclusively dependent on the canonical KFERQ motif, but can also be conferred by alternative linear peptide motifs and/or specific post-translational modifications (PTMs) that generate or mimic a KFERQ-like conformation recognized by HSC70.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged proteomic and biochemical approach. First, we will use our established CMA reporter system (KFERQ-Dendra) in LAMP2A-reconstituted mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs) to perform a mass spectrometry-based screen for endogenous proteins that co-purify with HSC70 and LAMP2A under CMA-activating conditions (e.g., serum starvation, trehalose), but lack a canonical KFERQ motif. Candidate proteins will be validated using CMA activity assays and lysosomal isolation. Second, we will use site-directed mutagenesis and PTM-mimetic mutants (e.g., phospho-, acetyl-, or ubiquitin-mimics) on known and candidate substrates to test if specific modifications can create a functional CMA-targeting signal. Finally, we will analyze the role of these non-canonical signals in Treg cells from our LAMP2A-restored mouse model to assess physiological relevance.
proposals.contribution: This work will fundamentally expand the understanding of CMA substrate targeting, potentially revealing a regulatory layer that allows for dynamic, context-specific degradation. It will provide a more comprehensive map of the CMA degradome and identify novel regulatory nodes that could be targeted to modulate CMA in disease states where its activity is compromised.
proposals.novelty: While the KFERQ motif is dogma, our systematic search for alternative targeting signals directly challenges this singular view, integrating proteomic discovery with functional validation in physiologically relevant cell types, including immune cells where CMA's role is emerging but its targeting logic is unknown.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of Cytosolic Chaperones and Co-chaperones that Recognize and Deliver Substrates to LAMP2A in Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a critical selective lysosomal degradation pathway for maintaining proteostasis, with established roles in aging, neurodegeneration, and immune function. The canonical model posits Hsc70 as the exclusive cytosolic chaperone recognizing the KFERQ-like motif in CMA substrates. However, the proteome-wide diversity of CMA substrates and the dynamic regulation of CMA under stress suggest the potential involvement of additional chaperones or co-chaperones in substrate recognition and delivery to LAMP2A, which remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, in addition to Hsc70, specific cytosolic chaperones and co-chaperones (e.g., members of the HSP40, HSP90, or HSP110 families) can directly recognize CMA-targeting motifs and facilitate substrate delivery to and translocation across the lysosomal membrane via LAMP2A.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach: 1) A functional siRNA screen targeting all known human chaperones/co-chaperones in a stable reporter cell line expressing a photoconvertible CMA substrate (KikGR-KFERQ), quantifying lysosomal delivery via flow cytometry. 2) Affinity purification-mass spectrometry (AP-MS) using immobilized, motif-containing peptides from canonical (GAPDH) and non-canonical CMA substrates to identify interacting chaperones. 3) In vitro reconstitution assays with purified lysosomes and recombinant candidate chaperones to directly test their ability to bind substrates and stimulate LAMP2A assembly and substrate uptake.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test the exclusivity of Hsc70 in CMA, potentially revealing a network of chaperones that confer substrate specificity and regulatory capacity to the pathway. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding how CMA selectivity is achieved for a broad proteome and may identify new regulatory nodes for therapeutic modulation in CMA-deficient diseases.
proposals.novelty: While Hsc70's role is well-established, a systematic search for alternative or auxiliary cytosolic chaperones in CMA has never been conducted. This directly challenges the long-held paradigm of a single exclusive chaperone and investigates the potential for chaperone specialization in this selective autophagy pathway.

proposals.title: Constitutive Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy is Essential for Proteostasis and Cellular Homeostasis in the Absence of Stress
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a well-characterized selective lysosomal degradation pathway, but its role is predominantly defined in response to stress (e.g., starvation, oxidative damage). It is unclear whether CMA operates at a basal, constitutive level and whether this activity is required for routine cellular housekeeping. Resolving this is critical for understanding the fundamental physiology of proteostasis and for interpreting the consequences of CMA decline in aging and neurodegenerative diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CMA is constitutively active at a basal level in mammalian cells, distinct from its stress-induced activation, and that this basal activity is essential for maintaining proteostasis and cellular homeostasis under normal conditions.
proposals.method: We will generate a novel, inducible LAMP2A knockout mouse model and cell lines to acutely ablate CMA. Using a combination of CMA reporters (KFERQ-PS-Dendra2), quantitative proteomics, and metabolomics, we will profile degradation flux and substrate pools in unstressed conditions versus after CMA induction (e.g., with 6-aminonicotinamide, a known CMA activator). We will employ single-cell RNA sequencing on T cells from these models, building on Dr. Cuervo's recent immune work, to identify cell-type-specific homeostatic signatures dependent on basal CMA.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish whether CMA is a constitutive homeostatic pathway, identify a unique set of basal CMA substrates, and reveal the cellular processes most vulnerable to loss of basal CMA. It will provide a foundational framework for distinguishing the homeostatic versus stress-responsive functions of CMA in health and disease.
proposals.novelty: While CMA's role in stress is established, its potential as a continuous, essential homeostatic mechanism has not been rigorously tested. This proposal moves beyond correlative studies of CMA decline to directly test the necessity of basal CMA flux using acute, inducible genetic models in vivo.

proposals.title: A Comprehensive and Quantitative Atlas of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Substrates Across Murine Tissues and Metabolic States
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a selective lysosomal degradation pathway crucial for proteostasis, metabolism, and immune function, as shown by its role in T cell aging and response to calorie restriction. However, the field lacks a systematic, unbiased definition of the full CMA substrate repertoire, limiting our understanding of its physiological roles and contribution to disease. A comprehensive substrate atlas is needed to move beyond candidate-based studies and elucidate the full scope of CMA's regulatory network.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CMA substrate repertoire is highly tissue-specific and dynamically regulated by metabolic state, and that a significant portion of substrates are not predicted by the canonical KFERQ-like motif, revealing novel non-canonical functions of CMA.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged proteomic strategy using established CMA reporter mouse models (e.g., LAMP2A-HA). Substrates will be isolated via lysosomal immunopurification and identified by quantitative mass spectrometry (TMT labeling). We will analyze multiple tissues (liver, spleen, brain) under basal conditions and following CMA activation (calorie restriction, pharmacological mimetics). Bioinformatic analysis will integrate proteomic hits with RNA-seq data and utilize machine learning algorithms (e.g., gradient boosting classifiers) trained on known KFERQ motifs and structural features to predict novel substrate determinants and validate hits via established CMA flux assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first quantitative, systems-level map of CMA substrates, providing an essential resource for the field. It will reveal novel metabolic and regulatory pathways under CMA control and identify tissue-specific vulnerabilities in CMA decline. The dataset will also enable the discovery of non-canonical substrate recognition mechanisms, expanding the functional understanding of CMA beyond proteolysis.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies identified individual CMA substrates, this proposal is novel in its systematic, unbiased, and comparative approach across tissues and conditions. The integration of advanced proteomics with machine learning to decipher recognition rules beyond the canonical motif represents a significant methodological advance over existing motif-scanning predictions.

proposals.title: Causal dissection of chaperone-mediated autophagy decline in the accumulation of α-synuclein and tau pathology in aging brain
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) activity declines with age, a process strongly implicated in neurodegeneration. Pre-2026 work by Cuervo's lab and others shows CMA degrades key pathogenic proteins like α-synuclein and tau, and its restoration improves cellular and organismal health. However, a direct, causal link between the specific age-related decrease in CMA and the progression of distinct proteinopathic markers in vivo remains unproven, limiting targeted therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the age-dependent reduction in CMA function is a primary driver of the selective accumulation of specific neurodegenerative disease markers (e.g., phosphorylated tau, oligomeric α-synuclein) in distinct neuronal populations, and that neuron-specific restoration of CMA will reverse these pathologies.
proposals.method: We will employ a conditional, inducible LAMP2A knockout mouse model to spatiotemporally inhibit CMA specifically in dopaminergic (TH-positive) or forebrain (CaMKIIα-positive) neurons in aged animals. Using quantitative immunohistochemistry and biochemical fractionation, we will correlate CMA activity loss with levels and solubility of α-synuclein and tau isoforms. We will then use AAV-mediated, neuron-specific LAMP2A re-expression (as in recent Cuervo lab work) to test reversibility. Proteomic analysis of CMA substrates isolated via the KFERQ-like motif affinity method will identify pathology-linked client proteins.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal relationship between CMA failure in specific cell types and defined proteinopathies of aging. It will identify novel CMA client proteins contributing to pathology, providing mechanistic insight. The findings will validate neuron-specific CMA enhancement as a precise therapeutic strategy for neurodegenerative diseases.
proposals.novelty: While CMA's role in degrading pathogenic proteins is known, this proposal uniquely tests causality by linking cell-type-specific, age-relevant CMA decline to distinct pathologies in vivo, moving beyond correlative studies in whole-tissue or systemic models.

proposals.title: High-Content Mapping of Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy Dynamics in Aging and Neurodegenerative Models
proposals.motivation: Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is crucial for neuronal proteostasis, and its decline is a hallmark of aging and neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's. However, current assays for CMA are largely endpoint biochemical measurements, lacking the spatiotemporal resolution to dissect the dynamic, multi-step process in live cells. This gap limits our understanding of where CMA fails in disease and how interventions like calorie restriction mimetics exert their benefits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-content, single-cell imaging will reveal distinct, quantifiable kinetic bottlenecks in the CMA pathway—from LAMP2A assembly at the lysosome to substrate translocation—that are differentially affected by aging, proteotoxic stress, and genetic risk factors for neurodegeneration.
proposals.method: We will adapt automated microscopy and image analysis pipelines from cancer drug discovery. Primary neurons and neuronal cell lines expressing fluorescent reporters for CMA components (e.g., LAMP2A-mCherry, KFERQ-Dendra2) will be subjected to high-content screening in 384-well plates. Using CellProfiler for segmentation and feature extraction, we will quantify CMA flux kinetics. Machine learning algorithms, specifically Random Forest classifiers, will be trained on extracted morphological and intensity features to automatically classify cells into discrete CMA functional states (e.g., 'efficient,' 'initiation-defective,' 'translocation-blocked').
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first dynamic, single-cell atlas of CMA functionality in neurons, identifying precise failure points in aging and disease models. It will provide a powerful platform for screening novel CMA modulators, directly extending from Dr. Cuervo's work on immune rejuvenation via LAMP2A restoration and the activation of CMA by calorie restriction mimetics.
proposals.novelty: While high-content screening is standard in cancer biology, its application to deconvolute the sequential steps of a selective autophagy pathway in neurons is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk CMA activity measurements to a granular, mechanistic dissection of its regulation at a cellular level.

ideas.title: Adaptor-Mediated Recruitment of CMA and Disaggregation Machinery for Aggregate Clearance
ideas.description: This redesigned approach tackles the fundamental substrate specificity barrier of CMA. It employs bifunctional adaptor molecules, with one domain binding to specific aggregate components (e.g., amyloid-β, α-synuclein) and the other containing a strong CMA-targeting motif, to forcibly localize LAMP2A and chaperones to the aggregate surface. Concurrent overexpression of CMA components (LAMP2A, HSC70) and a potent disaggregase complex works synergistically: the disaggregase partially fragments or unfolds the aggregate, while the recruited CMA machinery immediately engages and translocates liberated peptides into lysosomes. This creates a coupled 'disaggregate-and-degrade' system designed to clear pre-existing aggregates that are normally refractory to CMA.

ideas.title: Single-neuron mapping of LGP96 substrate binding motifs via CMA activity profiling
ideas.description: This research direction combines single-neuron CMA analysis with the structural definition of the LGP96 binding motif to create a high-resolution functional map. By introducing engineered substrate variants—each containing a systematically mutated putative binding motif—into individual dopaminergic neurons, one can directly measure how each mutation impacts CMA activity and substrate turnover at the single-cell level. This approach moves beyond in vitro binding assays to reveal how motif recognition governs substrate-specific CMA flux within the complex physiological context of a living neuron. Consequently, it can identify which motif features are essential for functional CMA engagement in health and how disease-associated mutations in LGP96 or substrates disrupt this process at a granular, single-neuron scale.

ideas.title: Dissecting mutant toxicity: A gain-of-function screen inspired by prion disease mechanisms
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that mutant toxicity arises solely from CMA inhibition by seeking a separable gain-of-function mechanism. The analogy comes from prion biology, where researchers solved the parallel problem of distinguishing the infectious prion conformation from the toxic species by identifying mutations that dissociated these activities. Adapting this solution, the research would employ genetic or pharmacological screens in a model system to find modifiers that uncouple mutant-induced cell death from the blockage of CMA flux. Identifying conditions that alleviate toxicity while CMA remains inhibited would provide direct evidence for a distinct, gain-of-function pathological pathway, fundamentally reframing the disease mechanism and opening new therapeutic targets beyond CMA restoration.

ideas.title: Computational prediction of pathological LGP96 substrate recognition using mutant-specific docking
ideas.description: This research fuses computational docking with systematic substrate discovery to create a predictive screen for pathological LGP96 substrates. Instead of solely docking a known mutant protein like alpha-synuclein, the approach will use protein-protein docking software to model interactions between LGP96 and a wide library of candidate cytosolic proteins, comparing wild-type versus disease-associated mutant forms of these substrates. This will generate a ranked list of proteins whose predicted binding to LGP96 is significantly strengthened or weakened by mutation, identifying new proteins whose aberrant lysosomal targeting could drive disease. The synergy transforms docking from a tool for analyzing a specific interaction into a discovery engine for identifying novel mutation-dependent degradation pathways.

ideas.title: Proteome-wide CMA substrate flux mapping to dissect gain-of-function toxicity from CMA inhibition
ideas.description: This fused idea proposes a comparative proteomic screen to identify all proteins whose degradation is inhibited under two distinct conditions: direct pharmacological or genetic CMA inhibition, and expression of a toxic CMA-related mutant. By comparing these two comprehensive substrate profiles, the study aims to isolate a specific subset of proteins whose accumulation is unique to the mutant condition. This mutant-specific signature would represent candidate mediators of a gain-of-function toxic mechanism, separable from the general consequences of CMA blockage. The approach thus directly tests the core hypothesis of Idea A using the scalable, unbiased methodology of Idea B.

ideas.title: Systematic Interactome Mapping to Identify Co-Receptors and Regulatory Factors in Mutant-Induced Lysosomal Blockage
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that receptor binding is the sole determinant of mutant-induced lysosomal blockage by adapting a solution from oncology, where therapeutic resistance was solved by moving beyond primary receptor interactions. The analogous solution involved systematic interactome mapping and genetic screens to identify the full network of modulating factors. Adapted here, the research would employ unbiased proteomics to define the mutant protein's interaction landscape at the lysosomal membrane and a targeted CRISPR screen to functionally test the requirement for other lysosomal membrane components beyond LAMP2A, thereby determining if the blockage is a simple binding event or a complex process requiring a multi-component assembly.

ideas.title: Single-Molecule Imaging of Competitive Substrate Displacement at the LAMP2A Pore
ideas.description: This project combines single-molecule TIRF microscopy of mutant alpha-synuclein binding to LAMP2A with a multiplexed, live-cell proteomic strategy to identify genuine CMA substrates. The synergy lies in using the single-molecule platform not just to observe the mutant blocker, but to directly test candidate substrates—identified via proteomic accumulation screens—in real-time competitive binding assays. This allows us to determine which substrates are actively displaced by the mutant, which can still bind but are trapped, and the kinetic parameters of this pore-level competition. The result is a mechanistic map of CMA blockage, distinguishing primary from secondary accumulation events and revealing the molecular hierarchy of substrate recognition at the lysosomal membrane.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Patch-Clamp Electrophysiology for CMA Activity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from cellular electrophysiology, where the patch-clamp technique isolates and records from individual cells, to the problem of measuring chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) activity in single dopaminergic neurons. The analogous solution of using a micropipette to create a high-resolution recording environment for a single cell is adapted by creating a microfluidic 'patch-clamp for biochemistry.' A single neuron would be isolated in a nanoscale chamber, allowing precise control over the local biochemical environment. CMA activity and the inhibitory effect of mutant proteins (e.g., alpha-synuclein) could then be measured in that single neuron using ultra-sensitive reporters for lysosomal uptake or degradation, providing unprecedented resolution on neuronal CMA heterogeneity and vulnerability.

ideas.title: Quantitative Kinetic Profiling of Mutant-Induced CMA Substrate Blockage
ideas.description: This study quantitatively tests whether CMA machinery mutations cause global or substrate-selective import defects by measuring the lysosomal translocation kinetics of a diverse, multiplexed substrate library. The library includes reporters engineered from different protein classes, varying in structure, stability, and motif context, to represent the physiological diversity of CMA substrates. Using live-cell imaging and biochemical fractionation with high temporal resolution, the import rate of each substrate is determined in isogenic wild-type and mutant cell lines. This kinetic profiling allows for the statistical identification of substrate classes that are disproportionately affected, moving beyond a simple yes/no blockage to a mechanistic understanding of mutant selectivity.

ideas.title: Single-Neuron CMA Profiling with Concurrent Functional Connectomics
ideas.description: This approach measures CMA activity and mutant blockage within individual dopaminergic neurons while simultaneously mapping the functional consequences on their synaptic transmission and local circuit activity. By using a paired recording and stimulation setup in brain slices, it correlates the intracellular degradative state of a neuron with its real-time output and influence on the network. This directly tests whether CMA dysfunction causes pathology primarily through cell-autonomous failure or through the disruption of specific circuit motifs, providing a mechanistic link between molecular blockage and systems-level dysfunction.

ideas.title: Magnetic fishing for blocked lysosomes using organelle-specific immunoprecipitation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the established solution of immunomagnetic separation from immunology/virology to solve the problem of isolating a specific subpopulation of blocked organelles. The analogous field uses antibody-coated magnetic beads to isolate specific cell types or pathogens from complex mixtures. The adapted research idea involves conjugating antibodies against a ubiquitous lysosomal membrane protein (e.g., LAMP1) to magnetic beads. When applied to a crude lysosomal preparation from cells expressing a mutation that blocks lysosomal function, all lysosomes will be captured. However, the key adaptation is that the magnetic pull-down will selectively enrich for the 'stuck' lysosomes, as the beads will also co-precipitate the large, blocked protein complexes associated with their luminal or membrane content. This enables the direct proteomic and biochemical analysis of the isolated, dysfunctional organelles to identify the nature of the blockage.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule Biophysics Techniques to Map the Complete Import and Degradation Pathway of a Lysosomal Substrate
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to detail the complete molecular steps of lysosomal substrate degradation. Drawing an analogy from single-molecule biophysics, specifically studies of nucleocytoplasmic transport through the nuclear pore complex, provides a methodological solution. In that field, techniques like single-molecule FRET and optical tweezers were used to map a cargo's entire journey, quantifying each recognition, translocation, and release step. Adapting this approach, the research would employ advanced single-molecule fluorescence microscopy and force spectroscopy to track a model lysosomal substrate (e.g., a fluorescently tagged protein) in real-time. The goal is to experimentally delineate and characterize every discrete event: from binding to the lysosomal receptor/scaffold complex (e.g., Hsc70), ATP-dependent unfolding, threading through the LAMP-2A translocation pore, to intra-luminal handoff to cathepsins and subsequent proteolysis. This would yield a kinetic and mechanistic map of the full import and degradation pathway, identifying rate-limiting steps and the mechanical forces involved.

ideas.title: Adapting Traffic Flow Models to Test if CMA Blockage Creates a Critical Bottleneck for Alpha-Synuclein Clearance
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving framework from traffic flow theory to investigate CMA's role in Lewy body formation. In transportation engineering, the formation of traffic jams is studied by modeling the relationship between vehicle density and flow to identify critical bottlenecks where congestion spontaneously arises. The analogous solution involves precisely defining the bottleneck, modeling the system to predict the critical impairment point, and then experimentally manipulating the bottleneck to observe the effect on flow/aggregation. Adapted to this research context, we propose modeling the neuronal proteostasis network as a traffic system, with CMA as a key clearance 'lane' for alpha-synuclein. The research would determine the critical level of CMA blockage (the 'bottleneck') required to cause a phase transition from normal flow to the 'traffic jam' of alpha-synuclein aggregation into Lewy bodies, moving beyond correlation to establish a direct causal threshold.

ideas.title: Proteome-wide CMA substrate mapping via multiplexed pulse-chase proteomics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology—multi-tracer stable isotope pulse-labeling for mapping species dependencies in food webs—to perform a proteome-wide screen for CMA substrates. The analogous solution involves introducing distinct tracers to track resource flow through an entire community. Adapted to cell biology, this translates to using multiplexed pulse-chase SILAC proteomics under conditions of CMA blockage. Cells in different isotopic states will be subjected to a CMA inhibitor at staggered times, followed by a chase period and quantitative proteomic analysis. This temporal multiplexing allows for the system-wide identification and kinetic profiling of all proteins whose degradation is inhibited due to the specific blockage of chaperone-mediated autophagy, effectively mapping the 'dependency network' of proteins on the CMA pathway.

Using single-molecule tracking and multiplexed fluorescence biosensors in live cells, it will measure the rates, efficiencies, and alternative fates (e.g., recycling, abortive disassembly) of each step from substrate binding to proteolysis. Concurrent optogenetic control of key nodes will test how the system adapts to perturbation, revealing its regulatory logic and vulnerabilities in different metabolic or stress conditions.

ideas.title: Decipher how cellular stress remodels the LGP96 substrate recognition motif to rewire signaling
ideas.description: This research direction integrates structural motif determination with expression dynamics to investigate how different cellular stresses (e.g., ER stress, viral infection, nutrient deprivation) alter the precise substrate-binding interface on LGP96's cytosolic tail. We hypothesize that stress-induced post-translational modifications of LGP96, changes in interacting co-factors, or tail cleavage events functionally remodel its recognition motif, thereby recruiting a distinct repertoire of signaling substrates. This would establish LGP96 not as a passive receptor with a fixed binding site, but as a dynamic signaling node whose connectivity is actively rewired by the cellular environment to direct specific stress-adaptive responses. The project will map the baseline binding motif and then systematically determine how it is modified under defined stresses to create a mechanistic map of condition-dependent LGP96 interactome switching.

ideas.title: Testing for Incomplete Proteolytic Processing Using Substrate-Specific Pulse-Chase and Mass Spectrometry
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all bound substrates are fully degraded by adapting a solution from enzymology. In metabolic studies, pulse-chase labeling with mass spectrometry is used to identify incomplete reactions and stable intermediates. The adapted research idea is to apply a substrate-specific pulse-chase protocol to a receptor-ligand or receptor-substrate system. By pulsing cells with an isotopically labeled substrate, allowing binding, then chasing with an unlabeled excess, one can immunoprecipitate the receptor complex at various time points. Subsequent high-resolution mass spectrometry analysis will be used to meticulously identify and quantify not just the intact substrate, but any persistent, partially processed fragments bound to the receptor, directly testing for incomplete proteolytic processing.

ideas.title: Ratiometric microfluidic assay for functional lysosomal delivery using co-encapsulated pH-reporting probes
ideas.description: This redesigned system employs droplet microfluidics to co-encapsulate single cells or purified lysosomes with two key fluorescent reporters alongside the cargo intended for lysosomal delivery. A pH-insensitive fluorophore is directly linked to the cargo to track its location, while a separate, freely diffusible pH-activatable probe serves as an independent sensor for entry into the acidic lysosomal lumen. By performing high-throughput, time-lapsed ratiometric fluorescence measurements within each droplet, the assay specifically identifies events where cargo fluorescence co-localizes with a spike in the pH-activatable signal, confirming functional delivery into the degradative compartment. This approach directly overcomes the critical flaw of measuring only association by providing a quantitative, internally controlled readout of successful lysosomal content exchange.

ideas.title: Real-time single-molecule tracking of bound substrate processing fate
ideas.description: This study will employ a substrate protein dual-labeled with donor and acceptor fluorophores at sites flanking the primary protease cleavage site. The labeled substrate will be introduced to its cognate receptor-degradation machinery complex immobilized for total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) microscopy. By monitoring smFRET signals in real time, the exact moment of cleavage (loss of FRET) can be detected and correlated with the subsequent dwell time and dissociation event of the remaining fragments. This approach directly tests the hypothesis by providing unambiguous, molecule-by-molecule evidence for complete degradation, partial processing, or intact release from the receptor complex.

ideas.title: Definitive discrimination between constitutive and cryptic inducible uptake via environmental invariance and systematic perturbation
ideas.description: This research tests the constitutive nature of a selective uptake pathway by first engineering an experimental system that minimizes hidden variables. A chemostat maintains cells in a steady-state of constant growth rate, nutrient levels, and waste removal, creating a stable baseline. The system then introduces a comprehensive panel of potential inducers—including metabolites, signaling molecules, and stress conditions—one at a time and in combinations, while using multi-omics to monitor for any coordinated change in the expression, localization, or activity of the pathway's components. This approach conclusively determines if the pathway is truly constitutive or if its apparent constancy masks responsiveness to previously unappreciated signals.

ideas.title: Investigate LGP96 regulation and expression dynamics using a synthetic biology feedback control circuit approach
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from synthetic biology and control systems engineering—the use of designed genetic feedback circuits to control protein expression—to investigate how LGP96 receptor activity and levels are controlled. The analogous problem of achieving precise, dynamic control over a system's output is solved by implementing synthetic negative feedback loops. Adapted to LGP96, this involves constructing a synthetic genetic module where LGP96 expression is placed under tunable self-repression. By introducing this module into cells and subjecting them to different conditions and stresses, we can directly observe and manipulate the regulatory dynamics in isolation. This approach will allow for the systematic mapping of LGP96's response logic, distinguishing cell-autonomous regulatory features from broader network influences, and providing a clearer understanding of its control mechanisms.

ideas.title: Iterative Phage Display Guided by Kinetic Fingerprinting of CMA Receptor Mutants
ideas.description: This project integrates the quantitative kinetic profiling of LAMP2A mutants (Idea A) with a directed phage display campaign (Idea B) to discover therapeutic CMA-modulating peptides. First, a panel of LAMP2A mutants is characterized to establish a detailed map of how specific structural changes affect binding affinity and dwell time for CMA substrates. This 'kinetic fingerprint' library then serves as the design blueprint for iterative rounds of phage display, where selection pressure is applied not just for binding, but for matching the precise kinetic parameters of a desired mutant. The synergy enables the de novo development of peptide inhibitors or activators with tailored on/off rates, moving beyond simple blockage to achieve predictable temporal control of CMA flux for research and potential therapeutic applications.

ideas.title: Ensemble-based flexible docking of mutant alpha-synuclein conformational states to LAMP2A
ideas.description: This redesigned approach first uses enhanced sampling molecular dynamics simulations to generate a realistic conformational ensemble for mutant alpha-synuclein, accounting for its intrinsically disordered nature. Multiple representative conformers from this ensemble are then used in parallel flexible docking calculations against the LAMP2A receptor. The resulting interaction profiles are integrated, weighted by conformational population, to predict the most probable binding mode and relative affinity changes caused by mutations. Finally, computational predictions of critical binding residues are tested experimentally via site-directed mutagenesis and binding affinity measurements to ground-truth the model.

ideas.title: Apply droplet microfluidics with fluorescence lifetime sorting for lysosomal uptake assays
ideas.description: This idea adapts a high-throughput screening solution from environmental microbiology to the problem of measuring lysosomal uptake. In the analogous field, droplet microfluidics and fluorescence-activated droplet sorting (FADS) are used to isolate single bacterial cells based on a metabolic activity signal. Here, we adapt this by using droplet microfluidics to compartmentalize individual cells or assays with fluorescent cargo. We will integrate a fluorescence-based readout—specifically sensitive to the acidic lysosomal environment (e.g., pH-dependent quenching or FLIM)—into the droplet stream. A FADS system will then sort droplets in real-time based on the measured lysosomal uptake signal, allowing for the high-throughput quantification of uptake kinetics and the physical isolation of subpopulations of cells for further analysis based on their lysosomal function.

ideas.title: Adapt Phylogenetic Character Mapping to Characterize Functional Evolution of LGP96 Homologs
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the method of phylogenetic character mapping from evolutionary biology to characterize the functional evolution of LGP96 homologs. The analogous solution involves tracing the evolutionary history of specific traits on a species tree to infer conservation and adaptation. The adapted idea is to identify LGP96-like proteins across a broad range of organisms, construct a detailed phylogenetic tree, and then systematically map key functional sequence features (e.g., domains, motifs) as characters onto that tree. This approach will move beyond simple sequence comparison to generate a functional evolutionary map, distinguishing the conserved core mechanistic components of the LGP96 pathway from lineage-specific functional modifications, thereby revealing the evolutionary pressures that have shaped this biological system.

ideas.title: Dynamic Pathway Modulation: From Constitutive Basal Rate to Feedback-Controlled Flux
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a selective uptake pathway operates at a constant basal rate by drawing an analogy from metabolic engineering and synthetic biology. In those fields, pathways once assumed to be static were redesigned to be dynamic through feedback loops and demand sensors. The adapted research idea proposes to investigate the uptake pathway not as constitutively active but as potentially modulated by hidden regulatory architecture. This involves experimental designs that probe for feedback inhibition from accumulated internal metabolites, activation by upstream signals, or responsiveness to changes in cellular demand, transforming the conceptual model from a fixed pipe to a regulated valve.
